about.metaDescription: Association of Victims Against Cyber-Scams (AVASC): mission, privacy-first design, and anti-scam support.
about.intro: AVASC is a nonprofit anti-scam platform built to support victims with dignity. We combine structured incident reporting, privacy-safe pattern intelligence, and practical recovery guidance.
about.do1: Help victims submit secure, structured scam reports.
about.do2: Analyze scam indicators and cluster recurring patterns.
about.do3: Publish anonymized public scam profiles for prevention.
about.do4: Issue <alerts>public scam alerts</alerts> and a weekly "This Week in Scams" briefing.
about.do5: Offer free <guides>scam-prevention education</guides> — guides, resources, and an AI Scam Check.
about.do6: Provide support-request workflows and recovery checklists.
about.not4: Not a place to publish private victim information.
about.privacyBody: Reports are private by default. Public database pages use anonymized, victim-safe summaries only. We do not expose private narratives, internal admin notes, or other victims' identifiable information in public views.

recovery.metaDescription: Find guidance and resources for recovering from cyber-scams.
recovery.title: Recovery center
recovery.intro: Grounded first steps and warnings — not legal advice. Pair this with a structured <report>scam report</report> so our team can triage and match patterns safely.
recovery.cryptoPoint1: Stop sending funds; rotate credentials if a platform was compromised.
recovery.cryptoPoint2: Export transaction hashes, wallet addresses, and chat logs (redact personal IDs).
recovery.cryptoPoint3: Report to your exchange and local cybercrime unit where available.
recovery.bankPoint1: Call your bank’s fraud line immediately with dates and amounts.
recovery.bankPoint2: Request recall / SWIFT investigation for wires when timing allows.
recovery.bankPoint3: Preserve emails, SMS, and call logs that show misrepresentation.
recovery.romancePoint1: Cease contact with the scammer; do not accept new “helpers” from the same thread.
recovery.romancePoint2: Document platform handles and payment trails.
recovery.romancePoint3: Seek emotional support from trusted people or counselors — shame is a tactic.
recovery.fakePoint1: Anyone promising guaranteed return for an upfront fee is highly suspect.
recovery.fakePoint2: Legitimate agencies do not ask for gift cards or crypto to “unlock” funds.
recovery.fakePoint3: Report the new scam attempt to AVASC with prior case reference if you have one.
recovery.evidence2: Indicators: domains, numbers, wallets, transaction IDs
recovery.evidence3: Screenshots and PDFs (upload after account creation where required)
recovery.resourcesBody: Filing with the relevant U.S. agency creates an official record, helps investigators spot patterns, and is often required by banks or insurers. Filing with AVASC does not replace these — we encourage you to do both.
recovery.ftcBody: reportfraud.ftc.gov — the U.S. Federal Trade Commission’s consumer fraud intake for scams, imposters, and deceptive business practices.
recovery.ic3Body: ic3.gov — the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center. File here for cyber-enabled fraud, wire fraud, crypto theft, and cross-border schemes.
recovery.identityBody: identitytheft.gov — FTC-run recovery planner that generates a personalized step-by-step plan if your identity or accounts were compromised.
recovery.storiesBody: Learn from others who've been scammed and recovered.
recovery.reportBody: Submit a detailed report to help identify scam patterns.
recovery.databaseBody: Look up indicators and check scam profiles.

report.phDescription: Timeline, what they promised, and what went wrong.

report.disclaimer: By submitting, you confirm the information is true to the best of your knowledge. AVASC is not a law firm or government agency.

guide_business_email_compromise.metaTitle: Business Email Compromise (BEC): How Scammers Target Companies
guide_business_email_compromise.metaDescription: Learn about CEO fraud, invoice manipulation, vendor impersonation, and W-2 phishing. Understand prevention policies and how to protect your organization.
guide_business_email_compromise.ogTitle: Business Email Compromise (BEC): How Scammers Target Companies | AVASC
guide_business_email_compromise.ogDescription: Learn how scammers use email to commit CEO fraud, invoice fraud, and other business crimes.
guide_business_email_compromise.h1: Business Email Compromise (BEC): How Scammers Target Companies
guide_business_email_compromise.intro: Business Email Compromise (BEC) is one of the most costly fraud types, causing billions of dollars in losses annually. Scammers impersonate executives, manipulate employees, and exploit company relationships to steal money and data. Understanding these tactics helps your organization defend against them.
guide_business_email_compromise.whatIsPara: Business Email Compromise is a sophisticated fraud attack where criminals send emails appearing to be from company executives, trusted vendors, or business partners. These emails request urgent money transfers, sensitive data, or credential changes. The requests seem legitimate and come from "inside" the company, making employees less suspicious.
guide_business_email_compromise.differsBody: Standard phishing attacks use mass emails with obvious red flags (poor grammar, generic greetings). BEC attacks are highly targeted and sophisticated. Scammers research company structure, understand business relationships, and craft emails that perfectly mimic an executive's communication style.
guide_business_email_compromise.type1Body: An email appears to be from the CEO or executive requesting an urgent wire transfer. The email cites time sensitivity: "Complete this before end of business," "Needed for acquisition," or "Confidential—don't discuss with others." Employees comply quickly without verifying through normal channels.
guide_business_email_compromise.type2Body: Scammers intercept or impersonate a vendor and send fraudulent invoices requesting payment. The invoice looks legitimate, includes company logos, and matches the vendor's format. Accounting departments pay invoices without additional verification.
guide_business_email_compromise.type3Body: Scammers send emails appearing to be from a trusted vendor requesting payment for invoices. They create email addresses nearly identical to the real vendor (vemdor.com instead of vendor.com). Employees pay invoices that never existed.
guide_business_email_compromise.type4Body: An email appears to be from HR or the CEO requesting W-2 information (Social Security numbers, salary information) for all employees. Scammers use this information to file fraudulent tax returns or commit identity theft.
guide_business_email_compromise.type5Body: Emails request urgent wire transfers to new accounts or foreign banks. The urgency and authority (appearing from an executive) pressure employees to bypass verification procedures.
guide_business_email_compromise.type6Body: An email appears to be from IT or a service provider requesting account credentials for "system updates" or "security verification." Once obtained, scammers access company systems, steal data, or commit further fraud.
guide_business_email_compromise.type7Body: Scammers compromise an employee's or executive's email account through phishing or password attacks. Once inside, they send fraudulent emails to colleagues, vendors, and partners that appear completely legitimate.
guide_business_email_compromise.researchPara: BEC attackers are thorough. They research your company extensively before attacking:
guide_business_email_compromise.research1: • Review your website for company structure, executives, and departments
guide_business_email_compromise.research2: • Check LinkedIn for employee information and organizational hierarchy
guide_business_email_compromise.research3: • Monitor social media for executive announcements and company news
guide_business_email_compromise.research4: • Research your vendors and business partners
guide_business_email_compromise.research5: • Review public financial documents and SEC filings
guide_business_email_compromise.research6: • Monitor press releases for mergers, acquisitions, and partnerships
guide_business_email_compromise.research7: • Check email directories and leaked password databases
guide_business_email_compromise.research8: • Study executive communication patterns from emails leaked online
guide_business_email_compromise.sender1: Email from an external domain instead of company email
guide_business_email_compromise.sender2: Similar but slightly different spelling (ceo-name@company-name.com instead of @company.com)
guide_business_email_compromise.sender3: Free email addresses (Gmail, Yahoo) used by "executives"
guide_business_email_compromise.sender4: Spoofed emails appearing to be from known contacts
guide_business_email_compromise.content2: Requests for secrecy: "Don't mention this," "Keep between us"
guide_business_email_compromise.content3: Unusual requests from normally routine transactions
guide_business_email_compromise.content4: Wire transfer requests to unfamiliar bank accounts
guide_business_email_compromise.content5: Requests for sensitive information (passwords, credentials, W-2 data)
guide_business_email_compromise.content6: Executive tone different from their usual style
guide_business_email_compromise.content7: Poor grammar or spelling (though sophisticated BEC attacks avoid this)
guide_business_email_compromise.process2: Requests to skip vendor verification procedures
guide_business_email_compromise.process3: Instructions to process payment immediately without review
guide_business_email_compromise.process4: Request to use unusual payment methods
guide_business_email_compromise.process5: New account information for a known vendor
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent1Body: Require MFA for all email accounts, especially for executives and finance staff. This prevents attackers from accessing accounts even with stolen passwords.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent2Body: For any financial requests, always verify by calling the requester back using a known phone number (not from the email). Never use contact information from the email itself. For vendor payments, verify new account information through a separate communication channel.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent3Body: Never allow one person to approve and process payments. Require at least two people: one to approve, one to execute. This prevents a single compromised employee from causing damage.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent4Body: Implement SPF (Sender Policy Framework), DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail), and DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication) to prevent email spoofing. These make it harder for attackers to impersonate your company.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent5Body: Regular security training helps employees recognize BEC attacks. Educate staff about the specific risks they face and the verification procedures your company uses.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent6Body: Establish a simple process for employees to report suspected fraud. Make reporting safe and anonymous so employees feel comfortable alerting leadership to threats.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent7Body: Use email filtering software to flag suspicious emails (spoofed addresses, external senders requesting payments, etc.). This catches many attacks automatically.
guide_business_email_compromise.prevent8Body: Limit what compromised accounts can do. For example, temporarily restrict email forwarding rules, disable automatic replies, and flag unusual account activity.
guide_business_email_compromise.suspectHeading: What to Do If You Suspect a BEC Attack
guide_business_email_compromise.suspect1Body: If you receive a suspicious email requesting payment, credentials, or sensitive information, stop immediately. Call the person at a number you know is correct (from the company directory or website, not from the email) and verify the request.
guide_business_email_compromise.suspect2Body: Alert your IT department and security team immediately. If an account has been compromised, they need to secure it. If wire transfers were made, they need to contact the bank immediately to try to recover funds.
guide_business_email_compromise.suspect3Body: If an employee's account has been compromised, reset their password immediately and force them to log out from all sessions. Require them to change passwords on other accounts that share the same password.
guide_business_email_compromise.suspect4Body: If funds have been transferred, contact your bank or financial institution immediately. Many fraudulent transfers can be stopped or reversed if reported quickly enough.
guide_business_email_compromise.suspect5Body: Report BEC attacks to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) at ic3.gov. Provide details about the attack, any funds lost, and the email addresses/accounts used.
guide_business_email_compromise.takeawayBody: Business Email Compromise is sophisticated and targeted. Your best defense is a combination of technical controls (MFA, email authentication, filtering) and human awareness (verification procedures, employee training). When unusual financial or sensitive data requests arrive via email, slow down and verify through an alternative communication channel. The few minutes spent verifying can save your organization hundreds of thousands of dollars.
guide_business_email_compromise.nextReportBody: Report suspected BEC attacks to help law enforcement.
guide_business_email_compromise.nextSearchBody: Check if an email address or company has been used in BEC attacks.
guide_business_email_compromise.footerNote: For detailed organizational security guidance, consult with a cybersecurity professional or contact the FBI's Cyber Division.

guide_charity_scam_verification.metaTitle: How to Verify a Charity and Avoid Donation Scams
guide_charity_scam_verification.metaDescription: Learn to identify fake charities, verify 501(c)(3) status, check Charity Navigator, and avoid being scammed by fraudulent organizations after disasters.
guide_charity_scam_verification.ogTitle: How to Verify a Charity and Avoid Donation Scams | AVASC
guide_charity_scam_verification.ogDescription: Learn to identify fake charities and verify legitimate organizations before donating.
guide_charity_scam_verification.h1: How to Verify a Charity and Avoid Donation Scams
guide_charity_scam_verification.intro: Charitable giving is meaningful—when your money actually helps people. Scammers exploit generosity by creating fake charities, especially after disasters when people want to help quickly. Learning to verify charities ensures your donations reach people who need them.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam1Body: Scammers create organizations with names nearly identical to legitimate ones: "American Heart Association" becomes "American Heart Fund," or "Red Cross" becomes "National Red Cross." Donors think they're supporting a well-known organization but are actually funding scammers.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam2Body: After hurricanes, earthquakes, wildfires, or other disasters, scammers create fake charities claiming to help victims. Emotional appeals and urgency lead people to donate quickly without verifying the organization's legitimacy.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam3Body: Someone calls requesting donations for a "charity" with a similar name to a well-known organization. Legitimate charities rarely cold-call for donations. These calls are scams designed to collect credit card information or bank details.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam4Body: Phishing emails appear to be from legitimate charities requesting donations or asking you to verify donation information. Links lead to fake websites that steal personal and financial information.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam5Body: Fake fundraisers on platforms like GoFundMe claim to help individuals or causes. The scammer collects money and disappears, leaving supporters with no way to verify the funds helped anyone.
guide_charity_scam_verification.scam6Body: Scammers use high-pressure sales tactics: "Donate now to help us meet our goal today," "This is our last chance to help," or "Your donation needs to be matched." This artificial urgency prevents you from verifying the charity's legitimacy.
guide_charity_scam_verification.section2Intro: Before donating, take a few minutes to verify the charity is legitimate:
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify1Body: Legitimate charities are recognized by the IRS as 501(c)(3) nonprofits. Search the IRS Tax Exempt Organization Search at irs.gov or the Charity Check tool. Verify the exact legal name of the organization and confirm it matches the charity contacting you.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify2Body: Charity Navigator (charitynavigator.org) rates charities on financial health and accountability. Look for organizations with high ratings and transparency. The site also flags problematic charities and scams.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify3Title: Check with the Better Business Bureau
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify3Body: The BBB Wise Giving Alliance (give.org) has information on thousands of charities, including their financial practices and governance. They rate charities as "accredited" or flag those with concerns.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify4Body: Search the charity's name on Google along with "reviews," "scam," or "complaints." Read what others say. Check sites like Trustpilot, Glassdoor (if they have employees), and news coverage.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify5Body: Don't call a number provided in an unsolicited call or email. Look up the charity's official website independently and find contact information there. Call them to verify they're fundraising and ask about their programs.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify6Body: Legitimate charities publish annual reports showing where money comes from and how it's spent. Look for charities that spend 75%+ on programs (not administrative costs). Transparency is a sign of legitimacy.
guide_charity_scam_verification.verify7Body: Check the domain name carefully. Fake charities use slightly misspelled domains or similar names. Look for secure websites (https://), professional design, and clear contact information. Scam sites often have outdated design or poor grammar.
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag1: • Pressure to donate immediately or make large donations
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag2: • No clear explanation of how money will be used
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag4: • Names similar to well-known charities but not identical
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag5: • Guarantees of tax benefits or returns on donations
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag6: • Requests to donate via wire transfer, cryptocurrency, or gift cards
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag7: • No physical address or contact information available
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag8: • Unwilling to provide financial statements or reports
guide_charity_scam_verification.redFlag10: • Claims that all donations go directly to recipients (legitimate charities have overhead)
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe1Body: Use trusted fundraising platforms (GiveWell, GlobalGiving, Network for Good) that vet charities. Your donation goes through a verified channel, reducing fraud risk.
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe2Title: Use credit cards or payment services
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe2Body: Donate with a credit card or through services like PayPal, which offer fraud protection. Avoid wire transfers, bank transfers, or cash, which are irreversible.
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe3Body: Save donation confirmations, receipts, and thank-you letters. These help verify the charity's legitimacy and are valuable for tax deductions.
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe4Body: Only donations to recognized 501(c)(3) charities are tax-deductible. If you're concerned, verify the organization's status with the IRS before donating.
guide_charity_scam_verification.safe5Body: After major disasters, scammers create fake relief organizations. Wait a day or two and donate to established charities with proven disaster response records (Red Cross, Salvation Army, established local organizations).
guide_charity_scam_verification.section5Heading: What to Do If You've Donated to a Fake Charity
guide_charity_scam_verification.donated1Body: Cancel any recurring donations immediately. Contact your bank or credit card company and request they block future transactions to that organization.
guide_charity_scam_verification.donated2Body: Contact your credit card company or bank and report the fraudulent donation. Explain that you donated to what you believed was a legitimate charity. Many will reverse the charge.
guide_charity_scam_verification.donated3Body: Report the fake charity to the FTC (reportfraud.ftc.gov), your state's attorney general, and the BBB. These agencies investigate charity fraud and can shut down fraudulent organizations.
guide_charity_scam_verification.donated4Body: Watch your credit card and bank statements for unauthorized charges. If you provided banking information, monitor accounts closely for fraud.
guide_charity_scam_verification.takeawayBody: Generous giving is a virtue, but scammers exploit that generosity. Taking a few minutes to verify a charity before donating ensures your money actually helps people. Legitimate charities are transparent, verifiable, and never use high-pressure tactics. When in doubt, donate to established organizations you know and trust.
guide_charity_scam_verification.nextCard1Body: Donate to a verified anti-scam nonprofit to help others avoid fraud.
guide_charity_scam_verification.nextCard2Body: Report fake charities to help protect other donors.
guide_charity_scam_verification.nextNote: Want to verify where legitimate charities get their funding? Check Charity Navigator, GiveWell, or the BBB Wise Giving Alliance for detailed financial reports.

guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.metaTitle: Common Cryptocurrency Scam Types and How to Avoid Them
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.metaDescription: Learn about pig butchering, fake exchanges, pump and dump schemes, and cryptocurrency investment fraud. Protect your crypto assets.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.ogTitle: Common Cryptocurrency Scam Types and How to Avoid Them | AVASC
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.h1: Common Cryptocurrency Scam Types and How to Avoid Them
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.intro: Cryptocurrency fraud is sophisticated, fast-moving, and often irreversible. Understanding the tactics—and how they differ from legitimate crypto—is essential for protecting your assets.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyHeading: Why Cryptocurrencies Are Targeted by Scammers
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyPara: Cryptocurrencies have become primary targets for fraud because they offer scammers significant advantages:
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyItem1Body: Unlike credit cards or bank transfers, crypto transactions can't be reversed or disputed.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyItem2Body: Transactions are difficult to trace, making it hard to identify or recover from scammers.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyItem3Body: Crypto is less regulated than traditional finance, giving scammers more room to operate.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyItem4Body: A scammer in one country can easily target victims worldwide.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.whyItem5Body: Transactions happen in minutes, giving victims no time to pause and verify.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigHeading: Pig Butchering (The Most Sophisticated Crypto Scam)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigPara: "Pig butchering" is an elaborate, long-game scam that can last weeks or months. The name refers to the process: raising a pig (building trust with the victim) then slaughtering it (stealing all their money).
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep1Body: Scammer reaches out via dating app, text, or social media, posing as an attractive person interested in dating or friendship.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep2Body: Over weeks, they build emotional connection, sharing personal stories and showing genuine interest in your life.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep3Body: They mention their "side business" of cryptocurrency trading or investment. They may show fabricated returns or profits.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep4Body: They ask you to join the investment opportunity, offering to help or guide you through the process.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep5Body: You're directed to a fake cryptocurrency exchange or trading platform. You deposit money, which scammers accept.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep6Body: The platform shows fake returns on your investment. Your balance grows in the app, but it's all fabricated.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigStep7Body: When you try to withdraw funds, you're told you need to pay fees, taxes, or make a larger deposit to "unlock" your returns. When you refuse, you realize the money is gone.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn1: Someone you met romantically suddenly mentions crypto investments
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn2: Offers to help you invest or guides you to a platform
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn3: Shows you screenshots of large profits from small deposits
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn4: Platform shows growing balance but you can't withdraw
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn5: They pressure you to deposit more to "unlock" returns
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pigWarn6: They claim fees or taxes are preventing your withdrawal
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExPara: Scammers create fake websites and apps that look nearly identical to legitimate exchanges. They're designed to steal your money or credentials.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook1: Professional-looking websites with branding similar to real exchanges
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook2: Apps that mimic Coinbase, Kraken, Binance, or other legitimate platforms
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook3: Slightly different URLs (coibase.com instead of coinbase.com)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook4: Charts showing fake market data and prices
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook5: Login screens that steal your credentials
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExLook6: Deposit functions that accept crypto but never reflect in your account
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerifyTitle: How to verify an exchange is real
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify1: Always type the URL directly into your browser; never click links from emails, texts, or messages
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify2: Check that the URL matches exactly (Coinbase is coinbase.com, not coibase.com)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify3: Verify the domain has an SSL certificate (look for the padlock icon)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify4: Download apps directly from the official app store, not from links in messages
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify5: Look up the exchange's official support contact and call them to verify
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.fakeExVerify6: Check if the exchange is registered and regulated in your jurisdiction
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpPara: Scammers promote obscure or newly created cryptocurrencies to drive up the price (pump), then sell their holdings for profit (dump), leaving regular investors with worthless coins.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpStep1Body: Scammers promote a worthless coin on social media, crypto forums, or Discord communities. They may pose as insiders, developers, or influencers.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpStep2Body: New investors buy the coin based on hype, driving the price up dramatically.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpStep3Body: The original scammers sell their coins at the inflated price, crashing the value.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpStep4Body: Late-arriving investors are left holding coins worth a fraction of what they paid.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpFlagsTitle: Red flags for pump and dump
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpFlag1: Promises of "guaranteed" returns from a new coin
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpFlag2: Pressure to invest quickly before the price rises further
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpFlag4: Coin suddenly appears in private groups or Discord chats
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.pumpFlag6: Coin with no clear use case or technology
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishPara: Scammers use phishing emails, fake websites, and malware to steal your crypto wallet credentials or recovery phrases.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishTactic1: Fake emails from Coinbase, MetaMask, or Ledger asking you to "verify" your account
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishTactic2: Links to fake wallet sites that steal your seed phrase or private key
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishTactic3: USB drives or downloads containing malware that monitors your wallet
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishTactic4: Social engineering to trick you into revealing recovery phrases
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishTactic5: Fake wallet apps that steal your credentials when you log in
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect1: Never share your seed phrase (recovery phrase) with anyone, ever
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect2: Never enter your seed phrase on any website, even if it looks official
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect3: Use hardware wallets (Ledger, Trezor) for large amounts of crypto
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect4: Enable all security features on your wallet (2FA, biometric lock)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect5: Only download wallets from official app stores or websites
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.phishProtect6: Never click links in emails about your wallet—go to the official site directly
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsPara: Scammers promise to grow your crypto through automated trading, AI bots, or exclusive investment programs. The platform accepts deposits but the "trading" is fictitious.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlagsTitle: Red flags for fake investment platforms
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag1: Guaranteed or unrealistic returns (20%, 50%, 100%+ per month)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag2: Pressure to invest large amounts quickly
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag3: Minimal or vague information about how the platform works
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag4: Fake testimonials from "traders" showing large profits
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag6: Difficulty withdrawing funds or excessive withdrawal requirements
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsFlag7: No regulatory oversight or licensing information
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsLegitTitle: What legitimate crypto investment looks like
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsLegit1: Clear, realistic returns (2-5% annually is more realistic)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.botsLegit6: Clear explanation of how returns are generated
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftPara: Scammers create fake NFTs or metaverse projects that are worthless or stolen artwork. Victims often lose significant amounts paying for assets that have no value.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftTactic1: Fake "collection drops" with copied artwork or stolen images
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftTactic2: Stealing NFTs directly from wallets using malicious smart contracts
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftTactic3: Impersonating legitimate projects (fake Discord or Twitter)
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftTactic4: Rug pulls: new projects that take investors' money and disappear
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nftTactic5: Fake metaverse land with no actual utility or value
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec1Title: Never invest in anything you don't understand
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec1Body: If you can't explain how an investment makes money, or the explanation doesn't make sense, it's likely a scam. Take time to research before investing.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec2Body: For buying and trading crypto, use major exchanges like Coinbase, Kraken, or Gemini that are registered with financial regulators. For holding crypto, use established wallets like MetaMask, Ledger, or hardware wallets.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec3Body: If someone is actively recruiting you into an investment or platform, especially through personal connections, it's likely a scam. Legitimate investments don't rely on recruitment.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec4Body: Your private keys and seed phrases are like passwords to your bank account. Never share them with anyone, type them online, or enter them on websites.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec5Body: If you're testing a new platform or investment, start with a small amount you can afford to lose. Verify that withdrawals work before depositing more.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.sec6Body: On all cryptocurrency platforms and wallets, enable 2FA using an authenticator app (not SMS when possible, as SMS can be compromised).
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.lostHeading: If You've Lost Crypto to a Scam
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.lostBody: Unfortunately, cryptocurrency transactions are typically irreversible. However, you should still report the scam to law enforcement and AVASC. Documenting the fraud helps authorities identify patterns and protect others. Some specialized recovery services may be able to assist with certain types of theft, but be careful—many "recovery services" are themselves scams.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nextDatabaseBody: Check if a crypto platform, person, or address is known to scam.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.nextReportBody: Document your experience and help protect other crypto users.
guide_cryptocurrency_scam_types.recoveryNote: If you've lost money to a cryptocurrency scam, visit our <link>recovery resources</link> for guidance on next steps.

guide_elder_fraud_prevention.metaTitle: Elder Fraud Prevention: Protecting Seniors from Scams
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.metaDescription: Learn about scams targeting seniors, tech support fraud, Medicare fraud, the grandparent scam, and safeguards for protecting older adults.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ogTitle: Elder Fraud Prevention: Protecting Seniors from Scams | AVASC
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.h1: Elder Fraud Prevention: Protecting Seniors from Scams
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.intro: Older adults are disproportionately targeted by scammers. Whether you're a senior yourself or protecting an aging parent, understanding these scams is essential for safeguarding finances and wellbeing.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyIntro: Scammers specifically target older adults for several reasons:
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyReason1Body: Seniors often have savings, retirement accounts, and home equity.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyReason2Body: Older generations were raised to be trusting and to respect authority, making them less likely to question or hang up on suspicious callers.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyReason3Body: Memory issues, difficulty with technology, or hearing loss can make some seniors more vulnerable to manipulation.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyReason4Body: Lonely seniors may be more susceptible to relationship-building scams (romance scams, fake grandchild calls).
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.whyReason5Body: Seniors may feel embarrassed and not report scams, making it harder for law enforcement to track patterns.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.grandparentBody: A scammer calls pretending to be a grandchild in crisis. "Grandma, it's me. I've been arrested/in an accident/need money for school. I need $5,000 by tonight. Don't tell mom and dad—I'm embarrassed."
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.grandparentFlag1: Pressure not to tell family members
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.grandparentFlag4: Caller asks not to verify with grandchild's parent
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.grandparentFlag5: Payment requested via wire transfer, gift card, or cryptocurrency
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.grandparentPrevent: Have a family code word. If someone calls claiming to be a grandchild, ask for the code word before sending money. Verify through other family members before sending any money.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.techBody: Pop-ups appear on the screen claiming the computer has a virus or security problem. A number to call appears. When the senior calls, scammers claim to be from Microsoft, Apple, or another tech company and request remote access to "fix" the problem.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.techFlag2: Phone numbers in pop-ups or emails
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.techFlag3: Requests for remote access or payment
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.techFlag4: Caller claims to be from Microsoft/Apple (these companies don't call)
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.irsBody: Phone calls or emails claiming to be from the IRS threatening legal action, arrest, or liens unless immediate payment is made. Often targets seniors around tax time.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.irsFlag1: Claims of unpaid taxes or penalties
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.irsFlag2: Threat of arrest or legal action
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.irsFlag4: Requests for gift cards or wire transfers
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.irsFlag5: IRS will NEVER call you; they send letters
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.medicareBody: Calls or letters claiming to be from Medicare or health insurance asking to "verify" Social Security numbers or Medicare numbers, claiming errors in records, or offering special health services.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.medicareFlag2: Claims of issues with Medicare or insurance coverage
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.medicareFlag3: Offers of free health services or equipment
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.medicareFlag4: Medicare won't call asking for personal information
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.lotteryBody: Notifications that the person has won a lottery they didn't enter or a prize, but must pay taxes or fees to claim it.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.lotteryFlag1: Winning a lottery you didn't enter
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.lotteryFlag3: Legitimate lotteries don't require upfront payment
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.romanceBody: A lonely senior meets someone online who quickly becomes romantic and then requests money for an emergency, travel, or investment opportunity. These can target isolated seniors of any age.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.homeBody: Door-to-door solicitors claiming to offer discounted home repairs or work for charities, then disappearing after payment or doing substandard work.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.homeFlag3: Requests for cash or upfront deposits
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.homeFlag4: Legitimate contractors have references and licenses you can verify
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect1Body: Talk to older family members about common scams. Approach the conversation with respect—many seniors don't appreciate being talked down to. Frame it as "these are tricks that fool even smart people."
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect2Body: Create a family code word that grandchildren, close friends, and trusted people use when calling in an emergency. If someone calls claiming to be a grandchild without using the code, it's likely a scam.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect3Body: Register phone numbers on the National Do Not Call Registry (donotcall.gov). While not perfect, it reduces unwanted sales calls that are often fronts for scams.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect4Body: If appropriate, offer to help review financial accounts, check bank statements, and monitor for unusual transactions. Watch for large unexpected transfers or wire transfers to unknown people.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect5Title: Use scam alert apps and services
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect5Body: Apps like RoboKiller or Nomorobo can block many scam calls. Many phone companies offer call screening services. Consider setting up a feature that requires unknown callers to identify themselves.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect6Body: Ensure seniors have direct contact numbers for family members so they can verify information if someone calls claiming to be a relative. A simple callback to a known number can prevent the grandparent scam.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.protect7Body: Help seniors update software, use password managers, and understand email verification. Tech support scams thrive on seniors' uncertainty with technology.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.legal1Body: A legal document granting someone trusted (usually a family member) authority to manage financial or healthcare decisions. This allows a trusted person to monitor accounts and prevent scams. Should be set up while the senior is mentally competent.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.legal2Body: A court-appointed arrangement where a guardian manages the legal and financial affairs of someone who can no longer manage their own. Used when someone is cognitively impaired. More restrictive and requires court involvement.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.legal3Body: Adding a trusted family member as a joint account holder allows oversight of spending and transactions, and prevents scammers from easily draining accounts. Discuss the implications carefully, as it affects both parties' assets.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.legalWarnTitle: Important: Financial safeguards require honest family involvement
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.legalWarnBody: While these tools protect from external scammers, unfortunately elder fraud is sometimes committed by family members. Safeguards should include oversight from multiple family members or trusted advisors when possible.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammedHeading: If a Senior Has Been Scammed
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammed1Body: Seniors often experience profound shame and embarrassment after being scammed. Reassure them that they're not alone and that intelligent people get scammed. Don't express anger or blame—they're already suffering.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammed2Body: The faster you report, the better chance of recovering funds or preventing further loss. Report to:
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammed2Item1: The senior's bank or financial institution
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammed3Body: Scammers sometimes sell lead lists to other scammers. The senior may receive increased scam attempts. Warn them about this and increase monitoring.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.ifScammed4Body: A therapist or counselor can help process trauma. Many seniors experience depression or anxiety after being scammed. Professional support is valuable.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.takeawayBody: Protecting seniors from scams requires balance. You want to help protect them from fraud without infantilizing them or damaging family relationships. Open communication, specific safeguards (like code words), and respectful oversight are more effective than control. Build trust with older family members about sharing concerns, and maintain that trust by involving them in protection decisions.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.reportCardBody: Document and report scams targeting seniors.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.recoveryCardBody: Guidance for seniors or families recovering from fraud.
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.resource1: National Adult Protective Services Association: www.napsa-now.org
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.resource2: Eldercare Locator: www.eldercare.acl.gov (find local elder services)
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.resource3: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau Senior Resources: www.consumerfinance.gov
guide_elder_fraud_prevention.resource4: National Center on Elder Abuse: ncea.acl.gov

guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.metaTitle: How to Identify a Scam: Warning Signs Everyone Should Know
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.metaDescription: Learn the common red flags, pressure tactics, and suspicious offers that indicate you may be targeted by scammers. Essential knowledge to protect yourself.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.ogTitle: How to Identify a Scam: Warning Signs Everyone Should Know | AVASC
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.h1: How to Identify a Scam: Warning Signs Everyone Should Know
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.intro: Scammers are skilled manipulators who study human psychology. They're patient, persistent, and increasingly sophisticated. But they share common tactics. Learning to recognize these patterns is your strongest defense.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.universalIntro: These red flags appear across almost every scam type. If you notice one or more of these, stop and verify before proceeding:
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign1Body: A stranger reaches out via email, text, social media, phone, or a dating app offering an opportunity you didn't ask for. Legitimate businesses rarely contact you unsolicited with financial opportunities.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign2Body: They ask you to pay via gift cards, cryptocurrency, wire transfer, or peer-to-peer payment apps. These are irreversible payments that scammers love because they can't be disputed once sent.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign3Body: "This offer expires today," "limited spots available," "act now before it closes." Artificial urgency prevents you from thinking clearly. Legitimate opportunities rarely demand immediate decisions.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign4Body: Guaranteed returns on investments, easy money for minimal work, free money you don't qualify for. If it sounds too good to be true, it is. Scammers hook you with the promise, then deepen the manipulation.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign5Body: "Don't tell anyone," "keep this between us," "your employer/family shouldn't know about this opportunity." Secrecy isolates you from people who could warn you.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.sign6Body: Poor grammar, spelling errors, unusual phrasing, or communication that doesn't match the organization's usual style. Scammers often operate from outside your country.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressureIntro: Beyond initial red flags, watch for these psychological manipulation techniques:
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressure1Body: They may spend weeks building rapport, asking about your life, showing interest in your family. This creates emotional connection before the scam deepens. The longer it lasts, the harder it feels to admit you've been fooled.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressure2Body: They start small ("send $500 to verify your account") then increase ("you need to invest $5,000 for the next tier"). Each small step feels manageable until you've committed significant money.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressure3Body: "Your account was compromised," "your taxes are in trouble," "your loved one is in danger." Panic prevents clear thinking and makes you more likely to send money without verification.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressure4Body: Pretending to be from your bank, the IRS, law enforcement, or a government agency. They use official-sounding language and may spoof phone numbers or email addresses.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.pressure5Body: "You're so smart, you'd be perfect for this investment," or targeting people who are lonely, grieving, or in financial difficulty. They identify emotional needs and exploit them.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.investment1: Guaranteed returns regardless of market conditions
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.investment3: Pressure to invest quickly in "exclusive" opportunities
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.investment4: Unregistered investments not listed on official financial registries
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.investment5: Promises of secrecy or tax advantages
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.romance1: Rapid declarations of love and commitment
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.romance2: Reluctance to video chat or meet in person
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.romance3: Story of personal crisis requiring financial help
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.romance4: Requests to move communication off the dating platform
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.romance5: Asking to transfer money "for their travel" or "business emergency"
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.crypto1: Promises of huge returns through crypto trading or mining
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.crypto2: Pressure to send crypto to a "secure wallet"
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.crypto3: Fake crypto exchange interfaces that look legitimate
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.crypto4: Offers of "free" crypto in exchange for personal information
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.crypto5: Celebrity or public figure endorsements of crypto schemes
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.techSupport1: Pop-up warnings claiming your device is infected
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.techSupport2: Unsolicited calls saying they're from Microsoft or Apple
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.techSupport3: Instructions to give them remote access to your computer
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.techSupport4: Requests to purchase gift cards to "resolve" issues
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.jobOffer1: Job offers requiring payment for "training" or "materials"
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.jobOffer2: Remote work opportunities with guaranteed high income
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.jobOffer3: Requests for personal information before an interview
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.jobOffer4: Job offers from companies you didn't apply to
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.jobOffer5: Offers to buy and resell items as part of the job
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.instinctsIntro: Your gut feeling exists for a reason. If something feels off—even if you can't identify exactly why—pause and verify. Some warning signs are subtle:
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.instinct1: • A person who seems perfect for you very quickly
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.instinct2: • An opportunity that arrived at exactly the right time
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.instinct3: • Communication that feels slightly unusual for the organization
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.instinct5: • Stories that shift or change slightly over time
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.verifyIntro: When something triggers a warning flag, here's how to verify:
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.verify1Body: Don't use contact information the person provided. Look up the official number or website independently. Call your bank directly (using the number on your card), visit the official government website, or contact the company through their main channel.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.verify2Body: If someone told you to keep something secret, that's a sign to tell someone. Share the situation with a trusted friend, family member, or advisor who isn't emotionally involved.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.verify3Body: Search for legitimate reviews, regulatory information, or complaints about the company or opportunity. Check if the investment is registered with the SEC or appropriate regulatory body.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.verify4Body: If you have to pay anything upfront to receive a job, inheritance, lottery winnings, or investment returns, it's a scam. Legitimate situations never require payment before you receive your money.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.takeawayBody: Scammers rely on your trust, your desire to help, your financial hopes, and your fear. They're skilled at what they do. Recognizing these warning signs isn't about being suspicious of everyone—it's about being thoughtful with money, relationships, and personal information. If something triggers a red flag, pausing to verify is always the right choice.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.nextCard1Title: What to Do If You've Been Scammed
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.nextCard1Body: Step-by-step recovery guidance and reporting resources.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.nextCard2Body: Check if a person, number, or company is known to scam.
guide_how_to_identify_a_scam.reportFooter: If you believe you've been scammed, please <report>report it to AVASC</report>. Your information helps us identify patterns and protect others.

guide_identity_theft_protection.metaTitle: Identity Theft Protection: How to Safeguard Your Personal Information
guide_identity_theft_protection.metaDescription: Learn how to protect your Social Security number, monitor credit, freeze your credit, detect data breaches, and monitor the dark web. Essential identity theft prevention.
guide_identity_theft_protection.ogTitle: Identity Theft Protection: How to Safeguard Your Personal Information | AVASC
guide_identity_theft_protection.ogDescription: Learn how to protect your Social Security number, monitor credit, freeze your credit, detect data breaches, and monitor the dark web.
guide_identity_theft_protection.h1: Identity Theft Protection: How to Safeguard Your Personal Information
guide_identity_theft_protection.intro: Identity theft can take years to recover from and costs victims thousands of dollars. But identity theft doesn't happen by chance—it happens because your personal information was exposed or stolen. Learning to protect your information and detect theft early is your best defense.
guide_identity_theft_protection.section1Intro: Identity thieves use multiple methods to steal personal information:
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card1Body: Hackers infiltrate companies' databases and steal millions of customer records. If you've been a customer at any major company in the past decade, your information may have been exposed. These breaches often aren't publicized until months later.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card2Body: Scammers pose as legitimate organizations to trick you into revealing personal information. They may call claiming to be from your bank, send emails requesting account verification, or create fake websites to capture credentials.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card3Body: Identity thieves steal mail, rummage through trash, break into homes, or steal wallets and purses to get documents containing Social Security numbers, account numbers, and identification.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card4Body: Using the same password across sites, connecting to unsecured WiFi networks, not updating software, and downloading malware can expose your information to thieves.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card5Body: Information is available in public records (voter registration, court documents, property records). Scammers compile this data and sell it or use it to commit fraud.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s1Card6Body: Once stolen, personal information is bought and sold on the dark web. Criminals purchase your data from previous breaches and other thieves to commit fraud.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s2Card1Body: Your SSN is the most valuable piece of personal information. Never share it unless absolutely necessary (employers, banks, credit agencies). Don't carry your Social Security card in your wallet. Be suspicious of anyone requesting it over the phone or online.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s2Card2Body: Protect your driver's license, passport, and state ID. Don't take photos of them or email copies unless absolutely necessary. Don't share photos on social media. Never give these numbers to unsolicited callers.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s2Card3Body: Never share bank account numbers, credit card numbers, or investment account numbers via email, text, or phone. Only enter this information on secure websites (look for the lock icon and "https").
guide_identity_theft_protection.s2Card4Body: Use unique, strong passwords for every account. Use a password manager to generate and store them. Never share passwords, even with family or friends. Be careful with security questions—scammers can find answers on social media.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s2Card5Body: This information can be used to open fraudulent accounts or file false insurance claims. Only share with medical providers directly. Don't post health information on social media.
guide_identity_theft_protection.section3Intro: Early detection is key. The sooner you discover identity theft, the sooner you can stop it:
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card1Body: You're entitled to one free credit report per year from each of the three credit bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) at annualcreditreport.com. Review them carefully for accounts you don't recognize, suspicious inquiries, or incorrect information. Get a fresh report every 4 months from each bureau.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card2Body: Watch your credit score through free services like Credit Karma or your bank's monitoring tools. A sudden drop can indicate fraud. Check your score monthly.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card3Body: Place fraud alerts with all three credit bureaus to be notified if anyone tries to open accounts in your name. These are free. You can renew them every year.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card4Body: A credit freeze prevents anyone (including thieves) from opening new accounts in your name. You can still access your own credit. Freezes are free and easy to set up with all three bureaus. Temporarily unfreeze when you're applying for credit.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card5Title: Monitor your bank and credit card statements
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card5Body: Review statements monthly (or more frequently) for unauthorized transactions. Enable account alerts for large or unusual transactions. Report fraudulent charges immediately to your financial institution.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card6Title: Monitor your email and account access
guide_identity_theft_protection.s3Card6Body: Watch for unexpected password reset emails, account confirmation requests, or login alerts from accounts you didn't access. These indicate someone may be trying to compromise your accounts.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item1: • Use strong, unique passwords for every account (12+ characters, mix of letters, numbers, symbols)
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item2: • Enable two-factor authentication on important accounts (email, banking, social media)
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item3: • Don't use the same password across multiple sites
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item4: • Use a password manager to generate and store secure passwords
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item5: • Don't click links in unsolicited emails or texts
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item6: • Don't connect to public WiFi for banking or sensitive transactions
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item7: • Keep your operating system and software updated with security patches
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item9: • Be cautious with public WiFi—use a VPN if you must connect
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item10: • Don't overshare on social media (birthday, hometown, pet names, etc. can be security answers)
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item11: • Verify website security before entering personal information (look for lock icon and HTTPS)
guide_identity_theft_protection.s4Item12: • Use your browser's password manager securely or a dedicated password manager
guide_identity_theft_protection.section5Heading: What to Do If You've Been Affected by a Data Breach
guide_identity_theft_protection.s5Card1Body: If a company you use has been breached, change your password immediately. If you've used the same password elsewhere, change it on all accounts.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s5Card2Body: Contact one of the three credit bureaus and request a fraud alert. They'll notify the other two automatically.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s5Card3Body: Check your credit reports immediately and then regularly for the next few years. Many identity theft attempts happen months after a breach.
guide_identity_theft_protection.s5Card4Body: After a significant breach, identity theft protection services may be offered for free. They monitor your credit and alert you to suspicious activity.
guide_identity_theft_protection.takeawayBody: Identity theft is a serious crime with long-lasting effects. The good news is that many preventive steps are free and simple. Protecting your most sensitive information, monitoring your credit, using strong passwords, and staying alert are your best defenses. If you suspect identity theft, act immediately—the faster you respond, the less damage occurs.
guide_identity_theft_protection.next1Body: Steps to take if you've been victimized by identity theft.
guide_identity_theft_protection.next2Body: Report identity theft to law enforcement and authorities.
guide_identity_theft_protection.relatedGuide: Learn about other types of fraud in our guide on <link>how to identify a scam</link>.

guide_investment_scam_red_flags.metaTitle: Investment Scam Red Flags: How to Spot Fraudulent Schemes
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.metaDescription: Learn to identify Ponzi schemes, unregistered investments, pressure-based tactics, and guaranteed return promises. Protect your money from investment fraud.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.ogTitle: Investment Scam Red Flags: How to Spot Fraudulent Schemes | AVASC
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.h1: Investment Scam Red Flags: How to Spot Fraudulent Schemes
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.intro: Investment scams prey on your desire to build wealth. Scammers promise returns that seem too good to resist, create false urgency, and use sophisticated deception. Learning to spot these schemes is your best defense.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.universalIntro: These warning signs appear across nearly every investment scam:
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf1Body: "Guaranteed 10% monthly returns," "You can't lose money," or "We've never had a losing year." No legitimate investment can guarantee returns. Markets fluctuate, and all investments carry risk.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf2Body: Returns of 20%, 50%, or 100%+ per year. For context, the S&P 500 averages about 10% annually. If an investment promises to consistently beat the market by 5-10x, something is wrong.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf3Body: "Limited spots available," "Closing to new investors next week," "You need to decide today." Legitimate investments don't require immediate decisions. This artificial urgency prevents you from thinking clearly.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf4Title: Vague explanations of how it works
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf4Body: When you ask how the investment generates returns, you get unclear answers, jargon, or promises to explain later. Legitimate investments have clear, understandable mechanics.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf5Body: Before investing, verify the company and investment are registered with the SEC (sec.gov) or FINRA (finra.org). Scammers often operate unregistered investments specifically to avoid regulatory oversight.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf6Body: You ask for written materials, prospectuses, or financial statements, but they're not available or are vague. Real investments have clear, comprehensive documentation.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf7Body: You're encouraged to bring friends or family into the investment, often with promises of commission or returns. This is a hallmark of pyramid schemes and multi-level marketing fraud.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf8Title: No clear conflict of interest disclosures
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.uf8Body: The person promoting the investment doesn't disclose how they benefit from your investment. Real financial advisors clearly explain fees and conflicts of interest.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.ponziBody: The most famous investment scam structure. Early investors receive their promised returns using money from new investors, not from actual investment profits. Eventually, there aren't enough new investors, the scheme collapses, and most people lose money.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.ponziSign1: Consistent returns regardless of market conditions
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.ponziSign2: Small, steady profits (not large ones that attract scrutiny)
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.ponziSign5: Difficulty withdrawing funds without investing more
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.affinityBody: Scammers target specific communities (religious groups, ethnic groups, professional associations) because people are more trusting within their community.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.affinitySign1: Investment is promoted through your church, club, or association
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.affinitySign2: Shared religious or cultural identity used to build trust
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.affinitySign3: Testimonials from people you know and trust (who may be deceived too)
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.affinitySign4: Promises that returns will benefit your community
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pumpBody: Scammers promote obscure, low-price stocks ("penny stocks") with false information, driving up the price. Once the price rises, they sell their shares at a profit, crashing the value and leaving regular investors with losses.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pumpSign1: Unsolicited emails or calls promoting a "hot stock"
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pumpSign2: Stock is little-known with minimal trading history
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pumpSign4: Pressure to buy before the price rises
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pumpSign5: Stock suddenly becomes hard to sell
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.forexBody: Scammers promise profits from currency trading (forex) or binary options (predicting if an asset price will go up or down). Most people lose money, and the platforms are often fraudulent.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.forexSign1: Claims of "high probability" or guaranteed profits
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.forexSign2: Exotic investment terminology designed to confuse
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.forexSign4: Platform withdrawal issues or refusal to return funds
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateBody: Scammers sell partnerships in real estate deals or promise returns from property flipping or rentals. The properties don't exist or the returns never materialize.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateSign1: Deals promising unusually high rental yields
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateSign2: Overseas property "opportunities" you can't easily verify
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateSign3: Difficulty getting property documents or viewing the property
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateSign4: Pressure to invest quickly or risk losing the deal
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.realEstateSign5: Promised returns don't match market reality
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.questionsIntro: These are critical questions that legitimate investments can answer clearly:
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q1Label: How exactly does this investment make money?
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q1Body: Be able to explain it in simple terms. If you can't understand it, you shouldn't invest in it.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q2Label: Is the investment registered with the SEC or FINRA?
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q2Body: Check finra.org or sec.gov. Legitimate investments are registered.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q3Label: Who is managing the money, and are they credentialed?
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q3Body: Verify their licenses and background. Check FINRA's BrokerCheck.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q4Body: Legitimate advisors disclose all fees clearly. Hidden fees are a red flag.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q5Body: Ask for audited financial statements, not testimonials. Real returns are documented.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q6Label: Can I easily withdraw my money?
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q6Body: If there are long lockup periods or restrictions, understand why. Be skeptical of investments that make withdrawal difficult.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q7Body: Legitimate investments clearly outline potential losses. Anyone saying there's no risk is lying.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q8Label: Can I get this in writing?
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.q8Body: Legitimate investments provide prospectuses and written agreements. Verbal promises alone are not sufficient.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify1Body: Go to brokercheck.finra.org and search for the advisor or broker. This shows their registration, credentials, disciplinary history, and any complaints. Avoid anyone not registered or with disciplinary records.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify2Body: Visit sec.gov and search the EDGAR database for the company's filings. Legitimate public companies file regular reports. Private companies offering investments should have disclosure documents available.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify3Body: Look up the company's physical address. Be suspicious of virtual offices, mail drops, or addresses that don't match their online presence. Call the main office independently to verify.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify4Body: Search the company name on complaint databases like the Better Business Bureau, state attorney generals' offices, and online reviews. Look for patterns of complaints.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify5Title: Talk to an independent financial advisor
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.verify5Body: Before investing, discuss the opportunity with a fee-only financial advisor who has no stake in whether you invest. They can review the offer and give you professional perspective.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.pressureIntro: Investment scammers use specific pressure tactics to overcome your hesitation:
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.fomoBody: "This opportunity is closing," "Only 5 spots left," "I can only hold this price for 48 hours." These artificial deadlines prevent thoughtful decision-making. Real investments don't disappear overnight.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.socialProofBody: "Everyone's investing in this," "My clients have made 50% returns," or testimonials from people claiming success. These may be fabricated or reflect only successful investors (not the many who lost money).
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.exclusivityBody: "This is only available to select investors," "I'm showing you this because I like you," "This isn't open to the public." Making something seem exclusive makes you feel special and important.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.bestResponseTitle: The best response to pressure: Always take time to think
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.bestResponseBody: If someone pressures you to decide immediately, that's a red flag. Legitimate investments will still be available tomorrow. Take time to research, ask questions, and sleep on the decision. If the pressure increases, that's confirmation it's likely a scam.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.takeawayBody: If an investment opportunity triggers any of these red flags, walk away. There are countless legitimate, safe investments available through established brokers and financial advisors. You don't need exotic opportunities with unrealistic returns. Your financial security is more important than the fear of missing out.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.searchDbBody: Check if an investment or company is known to scam.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.reportFraudBody: Document your experience and help protect other investors.
guide_investment_scam_red_flags.recoveryNote: If you've lost money to an investment scam, visit our <link>recovery resources</link> for guidance on reporting to authorities and potential recovery options.

guide_job_scam_warning_signs.metaTitle: Job Scam Warning Signs: How to Spot Fake Employment Offers
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.metaDescription: Learn to recognize fake job postings, work-from-home scams, and employment fraud. Protect yourself from upfront fee requests, check-cashing schemes, and reshipping scams.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.ogTitle: Job Scam Warning Signs: How to Spot Fake Employment Offers | AVASC
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.h1: Job Scam Warning Signs: How to Spot Fake Employment Offers
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.intro: The job market is competitive, and scammers exploit people's desperation to find work. They post fake job listings, offer unrealistic work-from-home positions, and request upfront payments for "training" or "materials." Learning to spot these red flags can save you money, time, and emotional distress.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section1Heading: The Most Common Job Scam Warning Signs
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section1Intro: Job scammers use similar tactics across platforms. Watch for these immediate red flags:
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign1Body: The biggest red flag. Legitimate employers never ask you to pay for training, materials, background checks, or "processing fees" before hiring you. If a job opportunity requires money before you start working, it's a scam.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign2Body: Legitimate job postings clearly describe responsibilities, required qualifications, and reporting structure. Scam postings are deliberately vague—"easy money," "work when you want," "no experience needed"—to appeal to desperate job seekers.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign3Body: "Make $10,000 a month working from home part-time!" is a classic scam pitch. If the salary is significantly higher than what similar jobs pay in your area, it's likely a scam designed to hook desperate job seekers.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign4Body: Legitimate employers conduct interviews to evaluate candidates. If you receive an immediate job offer without an interview, phone call, or video chat, it's a warning sign. Scammers skip interviews to avoid detection.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign5Body: If a company contacts you only via personal email, text message, or messaging apps instead of official company email or phone, be suspicious. Legitimate employers use professional communication channels.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign6Heading: Poor grammar and spelling in job postings
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign6Body: Professional companies proofread their job listings. Multiple spelling errors, awkward phrasing, and grammatical mistakes are often signs of a scam, especially if the job is supposed to be in English-speaking roles.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign7Body: If a major company is only advertising on classified sites, social media, or messaging apps rather than their official website or legitimate job boards, it's likely not a real position.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.sign8Body: Employers need your Social Security number for tax purposes, but only after you're hired. If they ask for banking details, Social Security number, or credit card information before you're officially employed, it's a scam.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type1Body: These prey on people seeking flexible work. The "job" might be stuffing envelopes, assembling products, or other tasks requiring startup fees. Once you pay, communication stops. Legitimate work-from-home jobs exist, but they're with established companies using official hiring processes.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type2Body: The "employer" sends you a check for supplies, equipment, or training. You deposit it and wire part of the money back as requested. Days later, the check bounces, and you're liable for the full amount. This is one of the most damaging job scams.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type3Body: You're hired to "receive packages and forward them." You receive items (often purchased with stolen credit cards), then send them elsewhere. You become part of the fraud chain and could face legal consequences.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type4Body: You're hired to evaluate stores or restaurants. After you pay an upfront fee or complete a fake assignment, the company disappears. Real mystery shopper jobs don't require upfront payments.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type5Body: You're offered a position but asked to pay a "processing fee" or "background check fee" before receiving official paperwork. Once paid, communication stops and you never hear from them again.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.type6Body: Scammers create fake websites or email addresses that look like they're from major companies (Amazon, Google, Apple, etc.). They post jobs on legitimate job boards using these fake identities to collect personal information.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section3Heading: How to Verify a Job Opportunity
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section3Intro: Before applying or providing any information, verify the job is legitimate:
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify1Body: Go directly to the company's official website (not a link provided in the job posting) and look for their careers page. If the job isn't listed there, it's likely fake. Be careful of fake websites that look similar to real ones.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify2Body: Look up the company's main phone number independently (not from the job posting). Call and ask if they're hiring for the position described. This is the fastest way to verify legitimacy.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify3Body: Use established job sites like LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor, or ZipRecruiter. Research the company on these platforms. Read employee reviews. Scammers sometimes post on these sites, but they're more likely to be caught and removed.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify4Heading: Look for the company on social media
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify4Body: Check the company's official social media accounts (LinkedIn, Twitter, Facebook). See if they post about job openings there. Legitimate companies have verified, active social media presences with many followers.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify5Body: Real company emails come from company domains (like @companyname.com). Scammers use free email services (Gmail, Yahoo) or near-identical addresses (like @companynamee.com). Check carefully.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify6Body: Search the company name plus "scam" or "complaints" online. Check sites like BBB (Better Business Bureau), ScamAdvisor, and Trustpilot. If many people report it as a scam, avoid it.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.verify7Body: This cannot be overstated: legitimate employers never charge job applicants for anything. Not for training, background checks, equipment, supplies, or processing fees. If money is requested before employment, it's a scam.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section4Heading: Red Flags During the Interview Process
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.section4Intro: Even if the job posting looks legitimate, be alert during the interview:
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag1: • Interviewer avoids video calls or phone calls, communicating only by email or text
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag2: • Interview is extremely brief and doesn't ask substantive questions about your qualifications
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag3: • Immediate job offer without discussing salary, benefits, or job responsibilities
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag4: • Request to send money before official paperwork is provided
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag5: • Vague answers when you ask specific questions about the role or company
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag6: • Request to work on a "trial project" and submit personal information or banking details
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.interviewFlag7: • Instructions to keep the job opportunity secret from others
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.takeawayBody: Job hunting is stressful, and scammers exploit that vulnerability. Remember: legitimate employers want to hire you, not take your money. If a job opportunity requires payment before employment, promises unrealistic income, or feels rushed and informal, trust your instincts and move on. The right job will come through proper channels without upfront costs.
