guide_job_scam_warning_signs.nextDatabaseBody: Check if a person, number, or company is known to scam.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.nextReportBody: Report a job scam to help protect others in your community.
guide_job_scam_warning_signs.recoveryNote: If you've already paid money to a scammer, don't give up. Visit our <link>recovery resources</link> for immediate steps to take.

guide_money_mule_awareness.metaTitle: Money Mule Scams: How Criminals Use Innocent People to Move Money
guide_money_mule_awareness.metaDescription: Learn what money mule scams are, how people get recruited, the legal consequences, and what to do if you've become involved in money laundering without realizing it.
guide_money_mule_awareness.ogTitle: Money Mule Scams: How Criminals Use Innocent People to Move Money | AVASC
guide_money_mule_awareness.ogDescription: Learn what money mule scams are, how people get recruited, the legal consequences, and what to do if you've become involved.
guide_money_mule_awareness.h1: Money Mule Scams: How Criminals Use Innocent People to Move Money
guide_money_mule_awareness.intro: Money mule scams turn ordinary people into unwitting participants in money laundering. Criminals recruit individuals to move stolen money, often by promising them work-from-home jobs or refunds. What many don't realize is that becoming a money mule can lead to serious legal consequences, even if you didn't know the money was stolen.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section1Para1: A money mule is someone who moves money on behalf of criminals, often without fully understanding they're breaking the law. Money mules use their bank accounts to receive stolen funds and then transfer them to other accounts, usually for a small commission. They're part of the criminal money laundering chain.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section1CardBody: Criminals steal money through fraud, ransomware, or theft. Moving stolen money directly is dangerous because banks and law enforcement track it. Instead, criminals recruit money mules to create distance between themselves and the money. The mule receives the stolen funds, takes a cut, and forwards the rest. By the time law enforcement investigates, the criminals are gone, but the mule is left with a bank account used for money laundering.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Para1: Money mule recruitment happens in multiple ways, often targeting vulnerable people:
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Card1Body: You see an ad for a work-from-home job that pays well and requires minimal work. The "employer" asks you to open a bank account (or use your existing one) to receive "payments from clients." Once set up, you receive money and wire it to another account, keeping a small commission. Unknown to you, the initial deposit is stolen money.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Card2Body: A scammer claims you're owed a refund from a company or government agency but the payment system is "down." They ask for your bank account details to "deposit the refund." You then receive deposits and are asked to wire back a portion for "processing fees." Again, the initial deposit is stolen.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Card3Body: A romantic interest you've been chatting with asks you to help them with a financial emergency. They ask you to receive money into your account and forward it to them. The money is stolen from fraud victims, and you've become part of the laundering operation.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Card4Body: You're hired as a mystery shopper and asked to deposit funds into your account to make purchases. You keep a commission and wire the rest back. The initial deposit is stolen money.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section2Card5Body: Instagram, Facebook, or TikTok ads target young people with promises of easy money for "financial transfer services" or "payment processing." These are money mule operations from the start.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Heading: Warning Signs You're Being Recruited as a Money Mule
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card1Item3: Work involves "receiving payments" and transferring money
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card1Item4: Emphasis on using your personal bank account
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card1Item5: Rapid hiring with no background check
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Title: The money transfer process feels off
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Item1: You receive deposits and are immediately asked to wire funds out
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Item2: You're told to keep commission and forward the rest
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Item3: No clear explanation of who the money is from or going to
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Item4: You're asked to use services that can't be reversed (wire transfers, cryptocurrency)
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card2Item5: Communication is casual and vague about the actual business
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card3Item1: You're told not to discuss this with friends or family
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card3Item2: Time pressure to set up accounts and receive/send money quickly
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card3Item3: Vague answers when you ask questions about the business
guide_money_mule_awareness.section3Card3Item4: You're told this is "normal" or "how business works"
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Para1: This is critical: becoming a money mule is illegal, even if you didn't know the money was stolen. The law holds you responsible for what flows through your account.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Card1Body: If you knowingly or unknowingly receive stolen money and transfer it, you can be charged with money laundering. This is a serious federal crime with penalties including 5-20 years in prison and fines up to $500,000.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Card2Body: Transferring stolen money via wire transfer is wire fraud, a federal crime punishable by up to 20 years in prison and fines up to $250,000.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Card3Body: Using your bank account to launder money is bank fraud, punishable by up to 30 years in prison and fines up to $1 million.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Card4Body: Your bank may freeze or close your account. The victims of the original fraud may sue you for losses. You may face asset seizure.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section4Card5Body: A conviction makes it extremely difficult to find employment, get loans, rent housing, or pursue education. The felony record follows you for life.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Heading: What to Do If You've Become a Money Mule
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Card1Body: If you suspect the money is stolen or the operation isn't legitimate, stop participating immediately. Don't transfer any more money. Don't answer calls from the recruiter.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Card2Body: Before reporting or taking action, contact a criminal defense attorney. An attorney can advise you on your specific situation and how to minimize legal exposure. Some jurisdictions offer cooperation agreements where you report and cooperate with authorities in exchange for reduced charges.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Card3Body: Contact the FBI, local police, or the Secret Service. Reporting the crime and cooperating with authorities may reduce your legal exposure. Be honest about what you did and why.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Card4Body: Inform your bank that you believe fraudulent activity occurred in your account. Your cooperation with the bank and authorities strengthens your case.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section5Card5Body: Save all communications (emails, texts, chats) from the recruiter. Document dates, amounts, and descriptions of what you were told and did. This evidence helps establish that you may have been deceived.
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Heading: Protecting Yourself from Money Mule Recruitment
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item1: Be skeptical of job offers that involve receiving and transferring money
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item2: Real jobs never ask you to use your personal bank account for company transactions
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item3: Legitimate employers conduct interviews and don't hire via social media ads
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item4: If an offer promises easy money for minimal work, it's likely a scam
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item5: Never accept money into your account from strangers
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item6: Be suspicious of anyone asking you to keep financial arrangements secret
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item7: Don't trust romantic interests who quickly ask for financial help or money transfers
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item8: Research any company before accepting employment (check BBB, reviews, official website)
guide_money_mule_awareness.section6Item9: When in doubt, ask trusted friends, family, or a financial advisor
guide_money_mule_awareness.takeawayBody: Money mule scams target vulnerable people with promises of easy money. They're designed to make you an unwitting participant in serious crime. Even if you were deceived, participating in money laundering carries severe legal consequences. If you've become involved, consult an attorney immediately. If you're being recruited, recognize the warning signs and refuse. Your account is your responsibility, and law enforcement holds you accountable for what flows through it.
guide_money_mule_awareness.nextReportBody: Report money mule recruitment if you've encountered it.
guide_money_mule_awareness.nextRecoveryBody: Get support and guidance if you've been involved in money laundering.
guide_money_mule_awareness.nextLegalNote: If you're facing legal issues, consult with a criminal defense attorney. They can provide legal advice specific to your situation and potentially help reduce your liability.

guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.metaTitle: Online Shopping Scam Prevention: How to Shop Safely Online
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.metaDescription: Learn to identify fake websites, counterfeit products, and unsafe payment methods. Shop safely with tips for verifying sellers and protecting your financial information.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.ogTitle: Online Shopping Scam Prevention: How to Shop Safely Online | AVASC
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.h1: Online Shopping Scam Prevention: How to Shop Safely Online
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.intro: Online shopping offers convenience, but it also creates opportunities for fraud. From fake websites that look identical to legitimate ones to counterfeit products and payment theft, scammers have become experts at deception. Learning to shop safely protects your money, your personal information, and your peace of mind.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam1Title: Fake websites that mimic legitimate retailers
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam1Body: Scammers create websites that look nearly identical to major retailers (Amazon, Target, Apple, etc.). The URL is slightly different (amaz0n.com instead of amazon.com), and the site collects payment information but never delivers products.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam2Body: Fake sellers list counterfeit products on Amazon, eBay, and other marketplaces. You think you're buying a genuine branded item but receive a cheap imitation. Sometimes they don't send anything at all.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam3Body: Someone posts luxury items (iPhones, designer handbags, electronics) at 50-80% off the regular price. They ask you to pay via bank transfer or cryptocurrency. You send money but never receive the product.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam4Body: You enter your credit card or bank information on a fake checkout page. Scammers capture this data and use it to make fraudulent charges. Your information may be sold on the dark web.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam5Body: Scammers send emails appearing to be from Amazon or another retailer, asking you to "verify your account" or "confirm payment information." Links lead to fake login pages where your username and password are captured.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam6Body: A marketplace seller receives your payment but ships nothing, ships the wrong item, or claims the package was "delivered" when you never received it. Some scammers disappear after opening shop and collecting deposits.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.scam7Body: You see an ad on Facebook or Instagram for products you like. The website looks professional, but it's a scam. After you pay, communication stops and you receive nothing.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.section2Intro: Before entering payment information, verify the website is legitimate:
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify1Body: Fake websites have URLs that are slightly misspelled (amz0n.com, amazom.com, amazone.com). Copy the official URL directly from Google or your browser bookmarks rather than clicking links from emails or ads.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify2Title: Look for the security lock and HTTPS
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify2Body: Legitimate shopping sites have a padlock icon in the address bar and URLs starting with "https://" (the 's' means secure). This indicates encryption is protecting your data. If you see "http://" without the 's' on a checkout page, it's not secure.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify3Title: Check for poor website design and grammar
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify3Body: Legitimate retailers invest in professional websites. If a site has poor grammar, spelling errors, low-quality images, outdated design, or inconsistent branding, it's likely a scam.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify4Body: Legitimate businesses provide clear contact information (address, phone number, email). Scroll to the bottom of the website. If there's no way to contact the company or the phone number is a VOIP service, it's suspicious.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify5Body: Check independent review sites like Trustpilot, Sitejabber, or Consumer Reports. Be skeptical if a website has no reviews or only 5-star reviews (fake sites often use fake positive reviews). Read negative reviews to understand problems.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify6Body: On Amazon, eBay, or other platforms, click the seller's profile. How long have they been selling? What's their rating? Do they have extensive history? New sellers with limited feedback and no history are riskier.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.verify7Body: If an item costs significantly less than everywhere else, it's a red flag. Search the item's price on multiple legitimate retailers. Major discounts of 50%+ are often scams.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.section3Intro: These practices significantly reduce your risk of being scammed:
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice1Title: Use credit cards or payment protection services
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice1Body: Credit cards offer better fraud protection than debit cards or bank transfers. Services like PayPal, Apple Pay, and Google Pay add an extra layer of security by not sharing your card directly with merchants.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice2Title: Never use wire transfers or cryptocurrency
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice2Body: These payment methods are irreversible and untraceable. Once scammers have your money, there's no way to get it back. Use these only for people and organizations you completely trust.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice3Body: Stick with established retailers and marketplaces (Amazon, Target, Walmart, Best Buy, Apple, etc.). These companies have reputation and legal liability, making them less likely to scam you. They also offer buyer protection.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice4Body: Turn on two-factor authentication for your shopping accounts. Even if scammers get your password, they can't access your account without the second verification.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice5Title: Verify seller ratings and return policies
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice5Body: Before buying from a marketplace seller, check their ratings and read recent reviews. Legitimate sellers have clear return policies. If there's no way to return items, it's risky.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.practice6Body: Save confirmation emails, receipts, and order numbers. If there's a problem, you'll have documentation. For major purchases, take screenshots of product descriptions and seller information.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.section4Heading: What to Do If You've Been Scammed
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action1Title: Contact your credit card company or bank immediately
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action1Body: Dispute fraudulent charges as soon as possible. Most credit cards have 30-60 days to report fraud. The sooner you act, the better your chances of recovery.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action2Title: File a complaint with the retailer or marketplace
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action2Body: If you bought through Amazon, eBay, or another marketplace, file a dispute. These platforms often side with buyers and can reverse fraudulent transactions or ban the seller.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action3Body: File a report at <link>reportfraud.ftc.gov</link>. The FTC collects data on fraud to identify patterns and pursue scammers. Your report helps protect others.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.action4Body: If you entered your shopping account password on a fake site, change it immediately. Use a strong, unique password. Also change passwords for email and other important accounts if they use the same password.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.takeawayBody: Online shopping can be safe if you stay vigilant. Before making a purchase, slow down and verify the seller and website. If something feels off—unusual prices, poor website design, no contact information—trust your instincts. The convenience of online shopping isn't worth the risk of identity theft or financial loss.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.nextDatabaseBody: Check if a seller or website is known to scam.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.nextReportBody: Report fraudulent sellers and fake websites to protect others.
guide_online_shopping_scam_prevention.recoveryNote: Scammed online? Visit our <link>recovery resources</link> for immediate steps to take and protect your financial accounts.

guide_phishing_email_protection.metaTitle: How to Protect Yourself from Phishing Emails and Fake Websites
guide_phishing_email_protection.metaDescription: Learn to spot phishing emails, verify senders, check URLs, and secure your accounts with two-factor authentication. Protect yourself from credential theft.
guide_phishing_email_protection.ogTitle: How to Protect Yourself from Phishing Emails and Fake Websites | AVASC
guide_phishing_email_protection.h1: How to Protect Yourself from Phishing Emails and Fake Websites
guide_phishing_email_protection.intro: Phishing is one of the most common ways scammers steal credentials, drain bank accounts, and gain access to sensitive information. The good news: you can learn to recognize and avoid phishing attacks.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Para1: Phishing is a scam where attackers impersonate legitimate organizations (banks, PayPal, Microsoft, Apple, etc.) to trick you into revealing passwords, credit card numbers, or other sensitive information. Most phishing happens via email, but it also occurs via text messages (smishing), phone calls (vishing), and fake websites.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Item1: Fake emails look nearly identical to real ones
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Item2: Attackers create artificial urgency ("Your account will be closed," "Suspicious activity detected")
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Item3: People often don't check URLs carefully
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Item4: Most people receive thousands of legitimate emails, making it easier to miss a fake one
guide_phishing_email_protection.s1Item5: Legitimate companies occasionally send similar emails, which confuses people
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card1Para: This is one of the most important checks. Legitimate companies email from their official domain. A fake from "paypa1.com" (with a 1 instead of l) or "secure-verification@banking.service" is not real.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card1ProTip: Hover over the sender's name in your email to reveal the actual email address. Some email clients hide the real address by default.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card2Para: Phishing emails often use generic greetings like "Dear Customer," "Dear User," or "Dear Valued Member" rather than your actual name. Legitimate companies know your name because they have your account information.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card3Para: "Your account will be closed in 24 hours," "Unusual activity detected," "Confirm your information now," "Your card has been compromised." These create panic that prevents careful thinking.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card4Para: Legitimate companies will NEVER ask you to verify passwords, credit card numbers, Social Security numbers, or other sensitive information via email. Period. If an email asks for this, it's a phishing attempt.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card5Heading: Links that don't match the text
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card5Para: Hover over any link in the email (don't click). Look at the URL at the bottom of your screen. If the link text says "Click here to verify your account" but the actual URL is "malicious-site.com," it's phishing.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card6Para: Professional companies proofread their communications. Emails with misspellings, awkward phrasing, or poor formatting are often phishing attempts. However, some phishing emails are well-written, so don't rely on this alone.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2Card7Para: Legitimate companies usually don't send unsolicited attachments. Be especially suspicious of Excel files, PDFs, or software downloads you didn't request. These often contain malware.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2RedFlagHeading: RED FLAG: "Click here to confirm" or "Verify now"
guide_phishing_email_protection.s2RedFlagPara: Any email asking you to click a link to "confirm," "verify," "update," or "validate" your account is likely phishing. Legitimate companies may ask you to log in, but they'll direct you to their official website, not via email links.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Intro: URLs are easy to fake. Here's how to check if a website is legitimate:
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card1Heading: Check the domain (the main part of the URL)
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card1Para: The real Coinbase URL is coinbase.com. Fake sites might use:
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card1Para2: Type the URL directly into your browser from memory or a trusted source. Never click links from emails or messages.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card2Para: Legitimate websites use HTTPS (secure connection). Look for a padlock icon in your browser's address bar. While HTTPS is standard, some phishing sites use it too, so this is helpful but not sufficient alone.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card3Para: Click the padlock to view the SSL certificate. It should show the legitimate company's name. If it shows a different name or a generic certificate provider, it's likely fake.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card4Heading: Go directly to the official website
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card4Para: Don't click email links. Instead, type the company's website into your browser or use a bookmark. This ensures you're visiting the real site. If there's an issue with your account, you'll see it when you log in directly.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s3Card5Para: Modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) have built-in phishing detection. They may warn you if you're about to visit a known phishing site. Pay attention to these warnings.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card1Para: Even if a scammer gets your password through phishing, they can't log in without a second factor (usually a code from your phone). Enable 2FA on:
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card1Note: Use authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) rather than SMS when possible, as SMS can be intercepted.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card2Para: Use a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden, LastPass) to generate and store unique passwords for each account. If one password is compromised through phishing, the others remain secure.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card3Heading: Never enter passwords on email links
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card3Para: Even if an email looks legitimate and asks you to "confirm your password," don't click the link. Log in directly to the website (type the URL yourself) to check if anything is wrong.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s4Card4Para: Monitor bank, credit card, and email accounts for suspicious activity. Many breaches go unnoticed for months. Early detection can prevent significant loss.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Heading: What to Do If You Click a Phishing Link
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card1Heading: You clicked the link but didn't enter information
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card1Para: You're likely fine. Just close the page immediately. Don't stay on the fake website. Monitor your email and accounts for unusual activity, but no sensitive information was compromised.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card2Para: Change your password immediately on the real website (not by clicking email links). Use a strong, unique password. Enable 2FA if you haven't already. Monitor your account for unauthorized activity.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card3Heading: You entered credit card, bank, or personal information
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card3Para1: Contact your bank and credit card company immediately. Report the fraud. They can:
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card3Item1: Monitor your accounts for suspicious transactions
guide_phishing_email_protection.s5Card3Para2: Also freeze your credit to prevent identity theft. Contact the three credit bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion).
guide_phishing_email_protection.s6Card1Heading: Report to the company being impersonated
guide_phishing_email_protection.s6Card1Para: Most companies have a phishing reporting email (usually phishing@companyname.com). Forward the phishing email to them (include the full headers if possible). They can take down fake websites and warn other customers.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s6Card2Para: Gmail, Outlook, and other email services have report buttons. Marking phishing emails as "phishing" helps improve their detection systems.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s6Card3Para: Report phishing and credential theft to reportfraud.ftc.gov. This creates an official record and helps law enforcement identify patterns.
guide_phishing_email_protection.s6Card4Para: Submit phishing websites to abuse databases like PhishTank.com. This helps browsers and security providers block the sites.
guide_phishing_email_protection.goldenHeading: Remember: Legitimate Companies Never Ask for Passwords via Email
guide_phishing_email_protection.goldenPara: This is the golden rule. Your bank, email provider, PayPal, Amazon, or any legitimate organization will never ask you to verify your password, Social Security number, or credit card via email. If they need to contact you about your account, they'll direct you to log in directly on their secure website, not through email links.
guide_phishing_email_protection.action1Desc: Document your experience and help protect others from the same scam.
guide_phishing_email_protection.action2Desc: Learn other warning signs and manipulation tactics.

guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.metaTitle: Romance Scam Warning Signs: How to Protect Yourself Online
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.metaDescription: Learn to recognize catfishing, love bombing, and financial manipulation in online dating. Protect yourself from romance scammers.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.ogTitle: Romance Scam Warning Signs: How to Protect Yourself Online | AVASC
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.h1: Romance Scam Warning Signs: How to Protect Yourself Online
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.intro: Romance scammers are masters of emotional manipulation. They study human psychology, exploit loneliness, and weaponize genuine emotion. Knowing what to watch for can save you heartbreak—and money.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section1Para1: Romance scammers move fast. In legitimate relationships, trust builds slowly. In scams, it happens at lightning speed:
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag1Body: "I've never felt this way before," "You're my soulmate," "I love you" within days of matching. Genuine relationships develop feelings gradually. Immediate declarations are a manipulation tactic.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag2Body: They match every interest you mention, have all the qualities you value, and seem designed specifically for you. Real people have quirks and contradictions. A profile that's too perfect is often stolen or entirely fabricated.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag3Body: They always have excuses: "My camera is broken," "I'm deployed overseas," "My company blocks video," "Bad internet connection." Video calls are the simplest way to verify someone's identity. Genuine people want to see you.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag4Body: Their job, location, family details, or background shift in subtle ways over time. They claim to be in one country then mention being in another. They forget details they told you before.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag5Title: Moving conversation off the platform quickly
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag5Body: Within days, they suggest moving to WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger, or email. Dating platforms have fraud detection and payment systems. Moving off-platform removes oversight.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.flag6Body: They constantly compliment you, make you feel special, and seem obsessed with getting to know you. While attention feels good, this level of focus is often calculated to deepen emotional investment.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section2Para1: Romance scammers use a specific psychological tactic called "love bombing"—overwhelming you with attention, affection, and promises to create deep emotional dependence:
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem1: Constant messaging and contact throughout the day
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem2: Rapid escalation of intimacy and commitment language
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem3: Promises of a future together (marriage, travel, shared home)
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem4: Using pet names and intimate language very quickly
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem5: Making you feel like you're the center of their world
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombItem6: Sharing personal stories (often fabricated) to deepen trust
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombPurposeBody: By making you feel uniquely loved and valued, the scammer creates emotional debt. You feel obligated to help them. When they later request money, saying no feels like rejecting the relationship. The stronger the emotional bond, the harder it is to refuse.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.loveBombVulnBody: After building deep emotional connection, they suddenly confess a crisis: "I'm stuck overseas and need money to get home," "My business has an emergency," or "I need help paying for my mother's surgery." The crisis feels urgent and personal.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section3Para1: Once emotional investment is deep, scammers introduce financial requests. These often escalate:
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finReqItem1: Money to cover travel to see you ("I can't get home to visit")
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finReqItem3: Business investment ("Help me start a business, then we can be together")
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finReqItem4: Legal fees for a custody battle
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finReqItem5: "Proof" of love in the form of gifts or cash transfers
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finEscalationBody: The first request is small. You send it. Then there's another. Each time you help, the next crisis seems slightly larger. The scammer has learned you'll send money, so the requests grow more aggressive.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finResistanceBody: When you hesitate to send more money, they may become emotional ("You don't really love me if you won't help"), disappear for a few days ("I'm upset and need space"), or introduce new "helpers" ("My lawyer/doctor/friend will explain").
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finWarningTitle: Critical warning: Multiple parties requesting money
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.finWarningBody: If the original person introduces lawyers, doctors, or "investment advisors" who also ask for money, this is almost always a scam. Legitimate professionals don't ask personal contacts for payment via wire or gift card.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section4Para1: Romance scammers often use specific personas because these backgrounds create plausible reasons for being unavailable or needing money:
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.persona1Body: Military personnel, oil rig workers, or international business professionals. The scammer uses this to explain why they can't meet in person, have poor internet, or need money sent internationally.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.persona2Body: A wealthy business owner looking for love. The irony: they're always having cash flow problems and need "quick loans." If they're truly wealthy, why would they ask you for money?
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.persona3Body: Single parent grieving a lost spouse. This creates sympathy and a reason why they're vulnerable and need support. Real widows and widowers don't immediately pursue new relationships with money requests.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.persona4Body: Their photos are often stolen from social media or modeling sites. If their profile photos look professionally done or too perfect, do a reverse image search.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section5Para1: If you're uncertain about someone you've met online, here's how to verify they're real:
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify1Body: Ask for a video call, not just a photo. Real people can do this. If they consistently refuse, they're not who they claim to be. When you video call, ask them to do something simple (like hold up an ID or move their hand) to prove it's live.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify2Body: Right-click their profile photo and select "Search Image with Google" or use tineye.com. If the photos are stolen, you'll often find them on modeling sites, stock photo sites, or other people's social media.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify3Body: Ask for their Facebook, Instagram, or LinkedIn. Real people have consistent online presence. Check the profile's history, posts, and whether they have real friends and interactions. New profiles or profiles with few followers are suspicious.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify4Body: Keep notes on what they tell you about their life. Do their stories change? Do details contradict? Do they forget what they said before? Real people's stories remain consistent.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify5Title: Never send money before meeting in person
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.verify5Body: This is the most important rule. If someone asks for money before you've met face-to-face, they're not interested in a genuine relationship. Full stop.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.section6Heading: If You Suspect You're Being Scammed
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.suspect1Title: Trust your gut, not your heart
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.suspect1Body: When facts and feelings conflict, believe the facts. A pattern of red flags matters more than what they say they feel. Your safety and financial security come first.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.suspect2Body: Share the situation with a friend or family member. Scammers often tell you not to. If someone is consistently telling you to keep secrets from people who love you, that's a major red flag.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.suspect3Body: If someone you've never met in person is asking for money, stop sending it immediately. It doesn't matter if they get upset, disappear, or threaten to end the relationship. Protecting yourself is more important.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.suspect4Body: Report the profile to the dating platform. Block the person on all platforms. Document evidence and file a report with the FTC (<link>reportfraud.ftc.gov</link>) and local police.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.reassuranceTitle: Important: You are not alone, and you are not stupid
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.reassuranceBody: Romance scammers target kind, trusting people who are looking for genuine connection. This is not a character flaw. The shame is on the scammer, not on you. If you've been victimized, please reach out for support and report what happened.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.actionDatabaseBody: Check if a name, photo, or username is known to scam.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.actionReportBody: Document your experience and help protect others.
guide_romance_scam_warning_signs.recoveryNote: If you're recovering from a romance scam, our <link>recovery resources</link> can help you navigate next steps.

guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.metaTitle: Sextortion and Online Blackmail: What to Do and How to Get Help
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.metaDescription: Learn about sextortion scams, why you shouldn't pay, how to report, and where to find emotional support. Guidance for victims and those protecting minors.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.ogTitle: Sextortion and Online Blackmail: What to Do and How to Get Help | AVASC
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.ogDescription: Learn about sextortion scams, how to report them, and where to find support.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.h1: Sextortion and Online Blackmail: What to Do and How to Get Help
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.intro: Sextortion is an extraordinarily distressing scam that uses shame and fear as weapons. If you or someone you know is targeted, know this: the shame belongs to the scammer, not to you. There is help available, and you don't have to pay. This guide provides straightforward information about what to do.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.whatIsPara1: Sextortion is a form of blackmail where scammers threaten to expose intimate images or compromising information unless you pay them. The scammers claim to have recorded explicit video of you, hacked your webcam, or obtained explicit photos. They threaten to send this content to your contacts, publish it online, or alert your employer.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.importantTruthBody: In the vast majority of sextortion cases, the scammers do NOT actually have videos or photos of you. This is a bluff designed to create panic. They send generic threats, often obtained from data breaches (your email appears in the message to make it seem credible). Even if they have your actual photos, paying them won't stop them, and it signals you'll pay more demands later.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.howMassBody: The most common form: You receive an email claiming the sender has compromising video or photos of you. The email includes your email address or password (obtained from a data breach) to seem credible. It demands payment (usually $1,000-$5,000 in cryptocurrency) or threatens to send the content to your contacts. These emails are sent to millions of people.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.howSocialBody: Someone adds you on social media, builds a relationship, then asks for intimate photos. Once you send them, they threaten to post them publicly or send them to your friends unless you pay. This is particularly common with attractive profiles used as lures.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.howDatingBody: A match on a dating app moves conversation to a private platform quickly, then asks you to video chat. During the chat, they claim to record you in compromising moments. They then demand payment or threaten to post on social media or send to your contacts.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.howDmBody: A scammer messages you on Instagram, Facebook, or other platforms claiming to have video of you. They may reference real details about your life (job, location, interests) found through social media stalking to seem credible.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.neverPayIntro: This is critical: paying will not help you. Here's why:
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.payMoreBody: Paying once proves you'll pay again. Scammers will continue demanding more money, threatening you repeatedly. Each payment escalates the demands. You'll be trapped in a cycle of extortion.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.noEvidenceBody: In most cases, they're bluffing. They use generic threats sent to millions of people. The inclusion of your email or a password from an old breach doesn't mean they have compromising content—it just means you're in a data leak. They're betting you'll panic and pay.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.evenIfTitle: Even if they have something, payment won't stop it
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.evenIfBody: If they do have intimate content, paying them won't guarantee they delete it or stop using it. You have no control and no recourse. Paying just makes you a victim of continued exploitation.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.fundingBody: Paying supports organized crime networks that victimize thousands of people. The money funds further scams and exploitation.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.whatToDoHeading: What to Do If You've Been Targeted
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step1Body: This is the most important step. Do not transfer money, buy gift cards, or send cryptocurrency. Paying will make things worse, not better.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step2Body: Don't respond to messages, don't negotiate, don't ask them to delete content. Communication signals that the account is active and might lead to more demands. Block the sender immediately.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step3Body: If the extortion came via email, report it as phishing/extortion. If via social media, report the profile. If via dating app, report and block. Most platforms have teams that investigate and remove scam profiles.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step4Body: Take screenshots of the threatening messages, the sender's profile, and any demands. Save these before reporting (reporting may delete the messages).
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step5Body: File a report with your local police department and the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) at ic3.gov. Include the screenshots, sender information, and any other details. Law enforcement is taking sextortion seriously and actively investigating.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step6Body: Change passwords on compromised accounts, especially email. Enable two-factor authentication. Check account activity for unauthorized access. If your password was in a breach, update it everywhere you've used it.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.step7Body: Telling someone—a friend, family member, counselor, or therapist—can reduce the shame and anxiety. They can provide support and help you see the situation clearly. Scammers rely on your silence and shame. Breaking that silence reduces their power.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.minorsHeading: Special Guidance for Minors and Parents
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.minorsIntro: If you're a minor being sextorted, or a parent concerned about a minor:
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.forMinorsBody: You are not in trouble. This is not your fault, even if you sent intimate photos willingly. The scammer is committing a crime by threatening you. Tell a trusted adult—a parent, counselor, teacher, or call a helpline. You deserve support, and adults who care about you won't blame you.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.forParentsBody: If your child is being sextorted, respond with compassion, not anger. They're victims of a serious crime. Listen without judgment, reassure them they're not in trouble, and help them take the steps above. Report to law enforcement and consider involving a counselor. Organizations like the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children (NCMEC) provide resources for minors being exploited.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.minorResource2: Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.supportIntro: Sextortion is psychologically devastating. Victims often experience shame, anxiety, depression, and feel isolated. Professional support can help:
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.therapyBody: A therapist experienced in trauma or online abuse can help you process the experience and recover. Many therapists offer online sessions, making it accessible. Your insurance may cover mental health care.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.hotline2: Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.communitiesBody: Many online communities connect sextortion victims who understand the experience and can provide mutual support. Knowing you're not alone can be powerful.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.notYourFaultTitle: Remember: This is not your fault
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.notYourFaultBody: Whether you were deceived, coerced, or your information was stolen, the blame belongs to the scammer. Victims of sextortion are not responsible for the criminal's actions. You deserve compassion—especially from yourself.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect1: • Be cautious about sharing intimate photos or videos with anyone, even people you trust
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect2: • Be skeptical of attractive profiles that move quickly to intimate conversations
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect3: • Never video chat with people you've just met online, especially if they ask you to remove clothing
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect4: • Use strong, unique passwords for all accounts
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect5: • Enable two-factor authentication on social media and email
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect6: • Limit what personal information is visible on your social media profiles
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect7: • Don't click links from unknown senders
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect8: • Be cautious of "romance scammers" who build relationships quickly online
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.protect9: • If something feels off, it probably is—trust your instincts
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.takeawayBody: If you're being sextorted, know this: you are not alone, you are not at fault, and you don't have to pay. The shame belongs to the scammer, not to you. Report to law enforcement, block the scammer, and reach out for support. Professional help is available, and recovery is possible. You will get through this.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.nextReportBody: Report sextortion and help protect others.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.nextRecoveryBody: Access support and guidance for recovery.
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.crisisTitle: Crisis support (if you're in immediate distress):
guide_sextortion_and_blackmail_scams.crisisBody:
Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741

National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: 988

These services are free, confidential, and available 24/7.

guide_social_media_scams.metaTitle: Social Media Scams: How to Stay Safe on Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok
guide_social_media_scams.metaDescription: Learn to recognize fake giveaways, impersonation accounts, marketplace fraud, QR code scams, and clickbait links on social media platforms.
guide_social_media_scams.ogTitle: Social Media Scams: How to Stay Safe on Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok | AVASC
guide_social_media_scams.h1: Social Media Scams: How to Stay Safe on Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok
guide_social_media_scams.intro: Social media platforms are ideal hunting grounds for scammers. Billions of users, limited oversight, and the ease of creating fake accounts and impersonating legitimate businesses make platforms ripe for fraud. Here's how to protect yourself.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem1Text: Billions of users with varying tech literacy.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem2Text: Fake accounts are simple to set up and difficult to verify.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem3Text: Unlike banks, platforms have minimal identity verification.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem4Text: Buying and selling features enable transaction fraud.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem5Text: People are more trusting within social connections.
guide_social_media_scams.whyItem6Text: Scammers can target specific demographics and interests.
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayIntro: One of the most common scams on social media. Scammers create fake versions of popular brands or celebrities offering "free" giveaways.
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayHow1Text: Create a page that looks like a popular brand (Apple, Nike, Amazon, etc.)
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayHow2Text: Promise a luxury item or cash ("Tag 5 friends to win a free iPhone 15!")
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayHow3Text: Users comment and share, spreading the scam
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayHow4Text: "Message us or click here to verify you're a real person"
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayHow5Text: Collect personal information, payment details, or malware
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot1: Check the page's verification status (blue checkmark on verified accounts)
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot2: Look at when the page was created (many giveaway scams are new pages)
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot3: Check the number of followers (fake pages often have low engagement despite large follower counts)
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot4: Read the comments (scammers often use fake accounts to comment positively)
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot5: Real brands rarely ask you to "click here" or "message to verify"
guide_social_media_scams.giveawaySpot6: If it seems too good to be true, it is
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayDoTitle: What to do if you interact with a fake giveaway
guide_social_media_scams.giveawayDoBody: If you've already commented or shared, change your passwords (especially if you use the same password across accounts). Monitor your accounts for unusual activity. Report the page to the platform.
guide_social_media_scams.imperIntro: Scammers create accounts pretending to be celebrities, influencers, or businesses to manipulate followers.
guide_social_media_scams.imperTactic1: Cloning a real account name with a similar username (e.g., @real_elon instead of @elonmusk)
guide_social_media_scams.imperTactic2: Creating an account claiming to be a celebrity "official account"
guide_social_media_scams.imperTactic4: Copying photos, bios, and content from the real account
guide_social_media_scams.imperTactic5: Messaging followers claiming to offer opportunities or asking for payment
guide_social_media_scams.imperIdTitle: How to identify real vs. fake accounts
guide_social_media_scams.imperId1Text: Look for the blue checkmark (though scammers sometimes fake this)
guide_social_media_scams.imperId2Text: Click on the account to see when it was created. Real accounts are usually older.
guide_social_media_scams.imperId3Text: Real accounts have diverse, authentic content and real engagement (not just bot comments)
guide_social_media_scams.imperId4Text: Real accounts grow gradually. Fake ones may have sudden follower spikes.
guide_social_media_scams.imperId5Text: Check if the bio matches the real person's known information
guide_social_media_scams.imperId6Text: Real celebrities rarely DM followers with opportunities
guide_social_media_scams.imperRuleTitle: Critical rule: Real celebrities and brands don't DM you with offers
guide_social_media_scams.imperRuleBody: If someone claiming to be a celebrity or influencer DMs you offering a job, money, or an exclusive opportunity, it's fake. Real opportunities come through official channels, not random DMs.
guide_social_media_scams.marketIntro: Facebook Marketplace, Instagram Shopping, and similar features enable buying and selling directly in the app. This creates opportunities for fraud.
guide_social_media_scams.marketCommon1Text: Buyer sends money, seller disappears without sending the item
guide_social_media_scams.marketCommon2Text: Seller offers luxury items at unrealistic prices, ships fakes
guide_social_media_scams.marketCommon3Text: Agrees to use the platform's payment system, then asks to pay via wire transfer, gift card, or crypto (to avoid buyer protection)
guide_social_media_scams.marketCommon4Text: Buyer sends more than the asking price and asks for a refund, but payment was fraudulent
guide_social_media_scams.marketCommon5Text: Scammers list properties they don't own, collect deposits, then disappear
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtectTitle: How to protect yourself on marketplace platforms
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect1: Use the platform's built-in payment system, never pay outside the app
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect2: Verify the seller (check their profile history, reviews, and how long they've been on the platform)
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect3: Be suspicious of unrealistic prices (if it's too cheap, it's probably fake or stolen)
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect4: Meet in person in a safe location for high-value items, or use local pickup
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect5: Inspect items before paying if meeting in person
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect6: For rentals, verify ownership and never wire deposits
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect7: Ask questions and request photos or videos if possible
guide_social_media_scams.marketProtect8: Don't accept payment beyond the asking price
guide_social_media_scams.qrIntro: Scammers post QR codes on social media that link to phishing sites, malware, or payment pages.
guide_social_media_scams.qrCommon1: "Free gift card, scan this QR code"
guide_social_media_scams.qrCommon2: QR codes in sponsored posts linking to phishing sites
guide_social_media_scams.qrCommon3: Codes that download malware or credential-stealing apps
guide_social_media_scams.qrCommon4: Codes linking to fake payment pages
guide_social_media_scams.qrCommon5: Overlay codes placed on top of legitimate QR codes
guide_social_media_scams.qrSafety1: Only scan QR codes from trusted sources
guide_social_media_scams.qrSafety2: Before opening the link, look at the URL preview in your camera app
guide_social_media_scams.qrSafety3: Don't scan QR codes from posts by unknown accounts
guide_social_media_scams.qrSafety4: Be suspicious of QR codes offering "free" items or money
guide_social_media_scams.qrSafety5: Never enter personal or payment information after scanning a QR code from social media
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitIntro: Sensational posts with links designed to get clicks, either for ad revenue or to spread malware.
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitCommon1: "Celebrities HATE this one trick..." (link to unknown site)
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitCommon2: "You won't believe what happened to [celebrity]" (fake news designed for clicks)
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitCommon3: "New healthcare/weight loss breakthrough" (often selling fake products)
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitCommon4: Posts promising to reveal embarrassing information about celebrities
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoidTitle: How to identify and avoid malicious links
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoid1: Hover over links (without clicking) to see the actual URL
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoid2: Be suspicious of sensational headlines designed to provoke emotion
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoid3: Check if the link is from a reputable news source
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoid4: Don't click links from posts with high drama or celebrity gossip
guide_social_media_scams.clickbaitAvoid5: Verify surprising news through multiple legitimate news outlets
guide_social_media_scams.romanceIntro: Social media makes romance scams easier by providing access to profiles, photos, and personal information.
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlagsTitle: Red flags for social media romance scams
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag1: Profile is very new or has minimal activity
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag2: Photos look professionally done or are too perfect
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag3: Person moves conversation off the platform quickly
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag4: They make immediate declarations of love or attraction
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag5: Hesitance to video chat despite claiming to want a relationship
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag6: Story changes or inconsistencies emerge over time
guide_social_media_scams.romanceFlag7: Financial requests appear after building emotional connection
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect1: Verify profile authenticity by checking account age and history
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect2: Do a reverse image search on their photos
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect3: Insist on video chatting early in the relationship
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect4: Be cautious if someone you just met asks for money
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect5: Discuss any suspicious relationship with trusted friends
guide_social_media_scams.romanceProtect6: Never send money to someone you've only met online
guide_social_media_scams.mlmIntro: MLM companies use social media heavily to recruit. While not all MLMs are illegal, predatory ones operate like pyramid schemes.
guide_social_media_scams.mlmWarning1: Pressure to recruit friends and family
guide_social_media_scams.mlmWarning2: Income primarily from recruitment, not product sales
guide_social_media_scams.mlmWarning4: Promises of easy money or "passive income"
guide_social_media_scams.mlmWarning5: Using social media heavily to recruit
guide_social_media_scams.mlmWarning6: Posts emphasizing recruitment over actual products
guide_social_media_scams.generalLimitBody: The more information available about you, the easier it is for scammers to manipulate you. Avoid posting:
guide_social_media_scams.generalLimit1: Your full birthdate (month and day are enough if sharing anything)
guide_social_media_scams.generalLimit4: Financial information or discussions about money
guide_social_media_scams.generalLimit5: Travel plans or vacation dates (shows when you're away)
guide_social_media_scams.generalPrivacyBody: Set your profile to private, control who can message you, and limit who sees your posts. Review privacy settings periodically as platforms change them frequently.
guide_social_media_scams.generalFriendBody: Scammers often clone accounts of people in your network or use fake profiles. If a request seems odd, reach out to the person independently to verify.
guide_social_media_scams.generalDownloadTitle: Don't download from links in messages
guide_social_media_scams.generalDownloadBody: If a friend sends a link to download something, verify with them first (via another method like phone). Their account may be compromised.
guide_social_media_scams.generalReportBody: Use the platform's reporting tools for fake accounts, phishing, and scams. While not immediate, these reports help platforms take action.
guide_social_media_scams.takeawayBody: Social media is designed to encourage quick reactions and sharing. Scammers exploit this by creating posts designed to provoke emotion without thought. Pause before clicking, sharing, or responding. A few seconds to verify can prevent significant harm.
guide_social_media_scams.searchDbBody: Check if a social media account, page, or person is known to scam.
guide_social_media_scams.reportScamBody: Document and report scams you encounter on social platforms.

guide_tech_support_scam_protection.metaTitle: Tech Support Scam Protection: Don't Fall for Fake Computer Warnings
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.metaDescription: Learn to identify fake virus popups, scam phone calls claiming to be Microsoft or Apple, and remote access tricks. Protect your devices and data.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.ogTitle: Tech Support Scam Protection: Don't Fall for Fake Computer Warnings | AVASC
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.h1: Tech Support Scam Protection: Don't Fall for Fake Computer Warnings
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.intro: Tech support scams are some of the most convincing and profitable scams in operation. Scammers create urgent-looking warnings that your computer is infected, then pressure you to call a fake support line or download remote access software. Understanding these tactics can save your device and your money.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Heading: The Anatomy of a Tech Support Scam
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Para1: Tech support scams work through a combination of fake warnings and psychological pressure. Here's how they typically unfold:
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Card1Body: You're browsing the web when a full-screen warning appears: "WARNING: Your computer has been infected with a virus!" The popup looks professional, often mimicking Windows or Apple warnings. It may play alarming sounds and prevent you from closing it easily.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Card2Body: The popup displays a phone number to call for "immediate support" or a button to download "antivirus software." Both lead to scammers. The phone number connects you to a fake support center; the download installs malware.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Card3Body: Everything about the fake warning screams urgency. "ACT NOW!" "Your data is at risk!" "Call immediately!" This panic is intentional—scared people make poor decisions.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section1Card4Body: If you call the number, a scammer pretending to be tech support will verify the "infection," use remote access software to show you fake problems on your screen, then demand payment ($300-500 typical) to "fix" everything.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card1Body: These appear while you're browsing websites (especially free content sites). They claim your device has viruses, spyware, or security threats. Apple and Microsoft never pop up warnings telling you to call a number—they provide warnings within their built-in security settings only.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card2Heading: Cold calls claiming to be Microsoft or Apple
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card2Body: A scammer calls claiming to be from Microsoft or Apple's support team, saying they've detected a virus on your computer. They ask you to give them remote access to "fix" it. Real companies never call unsolicited about viruses detected on your device.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card3Body: You download what appears to be antivirus software (often from an ad or fake warning), but it's actually malware. The fake antivirus then displays endless fake warnings to pressure you into paying for the "full version."
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card4Body: Scammers convince you to download remote access software (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, etc.) to "fix" your computer. Once installed, they have access to your files, passwords, and banking information. They may lock you out of your own computer.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card5Body: After "fixing" your computer and charging you, the scammer offers a "refund" but asks for bank account access to process it. They're actually stealing your banking information.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section2Card6Body: Popups claim your browser has "outdated plugins," "malicious extensions," or is "compromised." They pressure you to click links that either install malware or collect personal information.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Heading: How to Identify Fake Tech Support Warnings
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Para1: Legitimate security warnings have specific characteristics. Fake ones are designed to scare:
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item1: Real warnings: Come from your operating system (Windows, Mac) or apps directly, not from web popups
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item2: Real warnings: Don't include phone numbers to call or links to download software
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item3: Real warnings: Use your operating system's native interface, not flashy graphical designs
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item4: Fake warnings: Use alarming language and sounds to create panic
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item5: Fake warnings: Display phone numbers prominently ("Call now!")
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card1Item6: Fake warnings: Can't be closed easily or keep reappearing
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card2Body: Microsoft, Apple, and other legitimate companies do NOT call people unsolicited to alert them about viruses. If someone calls claiming to be tech support, it's a scam. Hang up immediately.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card3Body: Be extremely suspicious if someone asks for remote access to your computer. Once granted, they can access all your files, passwords, banking information, and emails. Never grant remote access to unsolicited callers.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section3Card4Body: Legitimate tech support may have a fee for professional help, but unsolicited tech support calls that demand immediate payment are always scams. Real companies don't pressure you to pay immediately via gift card or wire transfer.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Heading: What to Do If You've Encountered a Tech Support Scam
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card1Heading: If you see a fake warning popup
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card1Body: Don't click anything on the popup. Force-quit your browser: Press Ctrl+Alt+Delete (Windows) or Command+Option+Escape (Mac), select your browser, and click End Task/Force Quit. Restart your browser and check your security settings.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card2Heading: If you received a suspicious phone call
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card2Body: Hang up immediately. Do not give them remote access or any personal information. Block the number. Run a security scan on your computer to ensure no malware was installed during the call.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card3Heading: If you already gave remote access
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card3Body: Disconnect your computer from the internet immediately. Contact a trusted tech professional to scan your computer for malware. Change your passwords for all important accounts (email, banking, social media). Consider placing a fraud alert on your credit report.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section4Card4Body: Contact your bank or credit card company immediately to dispute the charge. Report the scam to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov. Follow our recovery resources for additional support and steps to take.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Heading: Protecting Your Devices from Tech Scams
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Para1: Prevention is always better than recovery. Take these steps to protect yourself:
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item1: Install legitimate antivirus/antimalware software (Windows Defender, macOS Security, Norton, Bitdefender)
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item2: Keep your operating system and software updated with the latest security patches
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item3: Use strong, unique passwords for important accounts
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item4: Enable two-factor authentication on critical accounts (email, banking, social media)
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item5: Be cautious on websites offering free content (movies, software, tools)—they often have malware
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item6: Don't click links in unsolicited emails or texts, even if they appear to be from legitimate companies
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item7: Use a password manager to secure your passwords
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item8: Disable pop-ups in your browser settings
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item9: Use browser extensions that block malicious websites
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.section5Item10: Remember: legitimate tech companies never cold-call about viruses
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.takeawayBody: Tech support scammers succeed because they create panic and urgency. If something on your computer makes you anxious, take a breath. Close any popups without clicking them. If someone calls, hang up. Contact tech support yourself—look up official numbers independently. Trust your instincts, and remember that real tech support companies don't use scare tactics or unsolicited cold calls.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.nextReportBody: Report tech support scams to help protect your community.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.nextRecoveryBody: If you've been scammed, immediate action can limit damage.
guide_tech_support_scam_protection.footerNote: Have questions about keeping your devices safe? Check out our guide on <link>how to identify a scam</link> for more warning signs to watch for.

guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.metaTitle: What to Do If You've Been Scammed: Step-by-Step Recovery Guide
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.metaDescription: Immediate actions to take after a scam, how to report to authorities, document evidence, and access emotional support. Recovery starts now.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.ogTitle: What to Do If You've Been Scammed: Step-by-Step Recovery Guide | AVASC
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.h1: What to Do If You've Been Scammed: Step-by-Step Recovery Guide
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.intro: Being scammed is not a personal failure. Scammers are skilled manipulators who exploit trust. The most important thing now is to take action. These steps will help you minimize damage and begin recovery.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.dangerBody: If a scammer is threatening violence or you're experiencing a mental health crisis, contact emergency services immediately.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Intro: Take these steps immediately to prevent further damage:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card1Body: Cut off communication with the scammer immediately. Block their number, email, and social media accounts. Do not respond, even to say goodbye or to ask questions.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card2Body: If the scammer has access to your email, banking, or social media passwords, change all of them immediately. Use strong, unique passwords and enable two-factor authentication on all critical accounts.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card3Heading: Check your accounts for unauthorized activity
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card3Body: Review your bank and credit card statements for unauthorized transactions. Check for new accounts opened in your name and monitor your credit report.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card4Body: Write down the wallet address and transaction ID immediately. Once crypto is sent, it's difficult to recover, but documenting this information is important for reporting and investigation.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card5Heading: If you sent a wire or bank transfer
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card5Body: Call your bank's fraud line immediately. Provide the transaction reference number, the recipient's name and account, and the wire amount. Your bank may be able to recall the funds if the receiving bank is also cooperative.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step1Card6Body: If you provided your Social Security number, driver's license, passport, or other identity documents, place a fraud alert with the credit bureaus and consider freezing your credit.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Intro: Gather and preserve evidence for official reports and investigations:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card1Item1: All messages (text, email, social media, dating app)
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card1Item5: Website or app screenshots showing fake offers
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card2Body: Write down chronologically when you met the scammer, how they contacted you, what they promised, when payments were made, and what you've learned since discovering the scam.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card3Item1: Amount of money sent and dates
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card3Item2: Payment methods used (gift cards, wire, cryptocurrency, etc.)
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card3Item3: Bank reference numbers or confirmation codes
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step2Card4Body: Write down any names (real or fake), phone numbers, email addresses, social media handles, dating app usernames, and any identifying information about the scammer. This helps authorities and other victims recognize patterns.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Intro: Multiple agencies need to hear about your scam. Reporting creates an official record and helps law enforcement identify patterns:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card1Body: This is the primary federal agency for consumer fraud. Report any online scam here, including romance scams, investment fraud, and crypto scams. The FTC shares information with law enforcement agencies.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card2Heading: FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) - IC3.gov
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card2Body: Report any internet-facilitated crime here, especially if significant money was lost. The IC3 is part of the FBI and investigates cybercrime patterns.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card3Body: File a police report with your local police department. Provide them with all documentation. You'll receive a case number, which may be needed for financial claims or credit disputes.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card4Body: Report fraud to your bank, credit card company, or payment service. They have fraud departments and may be able to dispute transactions or prevent further unauthorized activity.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card5Body: If you sent cryptocurrency, report it to the exchange or wallet service. While recovery is difficult, they may freeze suspicious accounts or assist law enforcement.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card6Body: Report your scam to AVASC. Your structured report helps us identify patterns, maintain a searchable database, and connect victims with targeted resources.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step3Card7Body: Report the fake profile or scammer account. They may have multiple targets. Include all documentation. These platforms sometimes cooperate with law enforcement.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Heading: Step 4: Protect Your Identity and Credit
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Intro: Take steps to prevent identity theft and credit fraud:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card1Item1: Get a free credit report from annualcreditreport.com
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card1Item2: Check for accounts you didn't open
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card1Item4: Sign up for free credit monitoring services
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card2Body: Contact the three credit bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) and request a fraud alert. This makes it harder for someone to open accounts in your name. You can place an initial alert by contacting any one of them.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card3Body: A credit freeze prevents new accounts from being opened in your name. It's free and can be placed with all three bureaus. You can lift it temporarily if you need to apply for credit.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card4Heading: Check your email and phone accounts
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step4Card4Body: Review login activity in your email and phone accounts. Look at connected devices and apps. Remove anything unauthorized. These accounts are gateways to all your other accounts.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step5Intro: Being scammed carries emotional weight. Shame, embarrassment, anger, and anxiety are normal. You deserve support:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step5Card1Body: Shame is part of scammers' toolkit—they exploit trust and compassion. Being fooled doesn't mean you're stupid or weak. Intelligent, kind people get scammed every day.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step5Card2Body: Tell a friend, family member, or therapist what happened. Isolation feeds shame. Honest conversation often reveals that others have experienced similar situations.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step5Card3Body: A therapist, counselor, or financial advisor can help you process trauma, make recovery decisions, and rebuild. Many offer sliding scale fees or online options.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step5Card4Body: Online communities and support groups for scam victims exist. Connecting with people who understand what you're going through can reduce isolation and provide practical advice.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6Intro: After you've been scammed, you're vulnerable to recovery scams. These are extremely common:
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6RedFlag1: Unsolicited contact offering to recover your money
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6RedFlag2: Requests for an upfront fee (gift cards, crypto, wire transfer)
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6RedFlag3: Claims of special government programs for scam victims
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6RedFlag5: Pressure to move quickly and keep it secret
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6Protect1: Never pay anyone for help recovering funds
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6Protect2: Real recovery is handled by law enforcement, not private services
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6Protect3: If someone contacts you with recovery help, they're probably scammers
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.step6Protect4: Trust legitimate agencies (FBI, FTC, your bank)
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.recoveryPossibleBody: Financial recovery depends on the type and speed of response, but emotional and psychological recovery is entirely within your control. By taking these steps, you're regaining agency and moving forward. Many victims find that their willingness to help others—by sharing their story, reporting to AVASC, or speaking up in their community—helps them heal.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.resourceIdentifyDesc: Learn warning signs to prevent future scams.
guide_what_to_do_if_youve_been_scammed.reportCta: You can also <link>report your scam to AVASC</link> directly. Our team will help ensure your case is documented and patterns are identified.

recoveryAi.title: AI recovery plan — tell us what happened
recoveryAi.intro: Describe your situation and we'll build a prioritized, step-by-step recovery plan — what to do first, where to report, and how to avoid being targeted again. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice.
recoveryAi.placeholder: e.g. I wired $5,000 to what I thought was my bank's fraud department after a call. I gave them a code from a text. What do I do now?
recoveryAi.warnPii: Do not include passwords, full card numbers, your SSN, or 2FA codes.
recoveryAi.genericError: Something went wrong. Please try again in a moment.

reportAi.title: Fill this in with AI
reportAi.intro: Just tell us what happened in your own words and we'll draft the fields below for you to review and edit. Nothing is submitted until you press submit.
reportAi.placeholder: e.g. I got a call from someone saying they were my bank's fraud team. They had me move $5,000 to a 'safe account' and read them a code from a text…
reportAi.filled: Draft added below — please review and edit before submitting.
reportAi.warnPii: Don't include passwords, full card numbers, or 2FA codes.
